author: L.K.
title: Good Lecture note + Good Instructors = Good Course
comment_content:
See attached “Lecture Notes.png”
Or you may go to the course website:
https://course.cse.ust.hk/comp2011
----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- -----

▽ Details

Part 1: Basics and Controls
Part 2: Function (Introduction)
Part 3: Array
Part 4: Recursion

Part 5: Scope
Part 6: Function III:
  - Function declaration
  - Function overloading
  - Default Function Argument

Part 7: Separate Compilation
Part 8: C++ Structure

Part 9: Pointers (Intro)
  - lvalue & rvalue
  - dereference a pointer
  - "const" in pointers initialization
  - Array as (constant) pointers

Part 10: Pointers (Usage)
  - Dynamic allocations (on Heap)
  - Problems on dynamic objects:
     ! Dangling pointer
     ! Memory leak
  - Linked List (LL)
  - LL + Binary Tree
  - Dynamic 1D and 2D array

Part 11: C++ Class (Intro)
  - Class == Struct with its own functions
  - keywords "public" and "private"
  - Constructors
     Trigger when a new object is created
  - Destructor
     Need when the object has pointers
  - Accessor
     Always followed by "const"
     Should NOT modify its data members
  - Mutator
     Opposite to accessors
  - Scope operator
     Syntax: <class_name>::
     Use to separate global function and
     member functions (Class scope)

Part 12: Stack and Queue
  - Stack: Last In First Out (LIFO)
  - Queue: First In First Out (FIFO)
  - Stack & Queue + Class
----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- -----

You may also download "confusion.pdf"
I listed out some of my confusion
when studying in this semester.

This will be divided into several parts:
confusion_1: Part 1-4 (Finished)
confusion_2: Part 5-6 (Finished)
confusion_3: Part 7-8 (In progress)
...and more

Hope this gives some help :)
comment_teaching:
The best thing in this course:
You can join any lecture that you like,
regardless of your enrolled section.

For me, I join both
Gary and Chatzopoulos's lectures.

Simple comments:
Gary → Build up a basic understanding
Chatzopoulos → Gives lots of practices

In my view,
they are both great instructors.
----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- -----

Gary给我的感觉有点像马云其实 XD
有时候上课滔滔不绝讲了十分钟，
总结起来就简单一句话：
「多练就会了」

而且前期他是在PDF上手写的，
时常遇到些问题，浪费了不少时间。

不过这并不妨碍我吹Gary，
他在讲解指针的时候特别的用心。

通过前期的教训，
后期的Gary每次上课都是
左半屏展示Notes，右半屏开白板。

然后在白板上一步一步地
画出指针的走向、应用。

真的很感谢Gary，
在指针上教的那么详细，
不然我期末很可能就死在指针上了。
----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- -----

Chatzopoulos真的是希腊人么……
我看着长得跟中东人似的（
（以下简称Chat）

在这个学期，
Gary的课堂是周一 + 周三（上午）
Chat的课堂是周三 + 周五（下午）

所以我每周都是刚上完Gary的课，
紧接着就是上Chat的课。

Chat平常讲课的节奏略快，
不过我此前听过Gary讲了一轮了，
所以对我来说听Chat的课就是在温书。

一般他会在课堂开头先整几个问题，
让我们用Zoom Poll（匿名投票）回答。

不论是匿名投票还是课堂讲解，
他的课堂都特别适合用来温书。

我也特别感谢Chat，
有些问题精准抓住了考点，
使我得以在期末避免犯低级错误。

总之Gary和Chatzopoulos的课
都有着不同的优点和特色。
本人强推这两位的课。
----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- -----

# Updated on 2022/04/09 #

焯！Chat跳槽了，亏我写了这么多。

As I know, Chatzopoulos is currently
teaching in other university. So...
This comment is useless now >.<

For the self-note, I'm not satisfied with
the existing notes and I'll rewrite them :)

comment_workload:
The workload is quite heavy
if your programming experience only
comes from course COMP1021 / 1022q.

---- 1. Lab ----

Some Labs are NOT easy.
(Especially for Lab 7 - 10)

Do recommend leaving at least one day
to finish the tasks in the lab.

---- 2. Programming Assignment ----

Tip 1:
Check the Assignment page regularly
to see if there are any updates.

Tip 2:
Always go to Piazza and ask
if you need more clarifications!

Tip 3:
Leave at least three days
to finish all the tasks in the assignment.
----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- -----

压力挺大的其实……
本人进这个课程之前完全没碰过C++

每次做Lab和PA都要花掉不少时间，
每周都要花将近6小时看两位教授讲解。

说实话这门课绝对值四学分。

你不但要了解讲课的大致内容，
还要钻研Notes里的每一个细节，
不然考试问到了你很可能就中招了。

而且说真心话，
有些考题简直就是脱离实际，
谁写代码天天塞堆foo和bar的……

这学期期末有一题在for loop里塞递归，
就是在恶心人，谁会这么写代码啊！

总之一句，放平心态，用心学。
Workload固然大，但也只能认了。

最后奉劝各位：
如果这不是你的必修课，慎入……
----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- -----

这Grade着实吓到我了……

考试考完我感觉最多B+（期末太难了）
出成绩感觉最多A-（因为PA1炸了）
结果出来是A

可能这就是人生吧.jpg

这门课好不好grade我真的不好说。
但就个人而言拿A还是挺不容易的。

尤其是期末的第一部分，
一来就是链表加二叉树，
差点把我心态整崩了。

还好接下来两个相比第一个都不算太难。

所以考试要放平心态考，
千万不要因为前面炸了就失去信心。

最后愿各位能把这门课当做一种享受。
毕竟不论考试，这门课从教材到教授，
质量都没的说。

如果你喜欢COMP1021的话，
可以考虑来这科试试。

不过你要做好心里准备：
递归、指针、链表、二叉树……
后期尽是些对新人不友好的东西。

但你还偏偏要熟练地运用这些东西，
因为这些东西期末考必出！
而且必出大题！

所以不是真心喜欢COMP1021的慎入，
因为这门课比COMP1021难得多！

感谢你能看到这里，
也希望这个评论会对你有所帮助。

author: LuckyTiger
title: Highly biased and critical review sorry
comment_content:
Sorry, but I am going to be highly biased and leave a critical review here, because a lot of reviews here make it seem like it's a pretty normal course. However, I am going to speak from the perspective of an average student who got a B in COMP1021 and had no prior experience in C++ before this course. I also have a victim mindset from this course.

This semester they didn't go through in detail the 'basic' lecture notes because apparently, people were complaining that it was apparently too boring. If I remember correctly, they told us to self-read these lecture notes one at a time before each next lecture and skimmed through during lecture time.

http://2011video.cse.ust.hk/videos/list.html

People may question why I went through these videos, but the lecture notes are almost the same and it's from about 10 years ago. 10 years ago Prof Mak took 5+ hours of quality lecture time to introduce C++ while these instructors told us to read it. I guess I am being dramatic because these beginner materials are not hard at all and they did go through it briefly. Oh well, but there's more shit to self-study later on.

The other lecture topics, aside from those in the link

Problems for Basics and Control
Function I
Array
Function II: Recursion
Scope
Function III: Miscellaneous
Separate Compilation
Struct

Pointer
A YouTube video single-handedly made me understand linked list, a sub-topic of this pointer topic.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=o5wJkJJpKtM&ab_channel=PaulProgramming

C++ Class
Stack & Queue

Self Study Topics ( for one lab ) ( not tested in exam )
File I/O
String

For each had optional tutorials.
For me I attended both simultaneously because of time management problems, but they weren't that helpful either. But I don't know, the information given in the lecture notes were not that helpful for the lab either.
comment_teaching:
To be honest, Professor Li is may be passionate. If you think about some instructors, they are indeed capable enough to transmit this curiosity and passion to students. My opinion: Professor Li unfortunately did not have this effect on me.

An immense number of times in lectures, she would go on and on about some part. Her monologues were detailed, but not concise. One thing that would tremendously improve her lecture is live speech-to-text conversion because my attention span could not remember the run-on sentences that she articulated with. She does have a plethora of knowledge to share.

A fact: at the last class, there were less than 10 students in the face-to-face lectures. Around 15 students on zoom. And I still wonder to this day how the were the other 65 students were doing.

I honestly did not know how to attend her lectures for the first half of the semester. After continuous effort going to lectures with a notebook and pen, seeking to draw some clear points being productive and shit... nearing the end of sem, I just sat and listened to her talk. It was actually much better because I got to at least focus on her words more clearly.

It seems like they are recording the lecture but they are not published. Then what's the point of recording it??
Please, I beg you, please, can you please, could you just make it easier for students to access these  information not just for students who are not in HK if you recorded it please?

An D to the teaching at the end because of the culture that they spread, which is honestly not even about plagiarism but about collaboration. They are so strict with plagiarism that they don't allow you to get explicit help from anyone in this course, which sucks, because I was stuck at certain tasks for days. Collaboration is pretty much discouraged. I mean, truth be told, it didn't really affect me because I had no friends but at the same time, I felt very reluctant to ask for help as I thought the help I would get would not be specific enough or whatever. I felt isolated. My opinion.
comment_grading:
Grading? I don't have anything to say. It's fair, for the ability you show, not necessarily the time you spend. That is sort of true with every course and that's no revelation.

I legit couldn't care any less about the so called competition in this course because the only competition there is, is for those smart people who wanna get A-range. I just wanted to get a B or a C.

Most of the contents of the exam seems to focus on the big, later lectures including pointers, stack/queue, class, struct. Recursion and array are  important too. The broadness of topics is actually not bad.

Realistic stats

Quiz 25/35 Mean: 27 or 28
Finals 26/100, mean: 49/100 or something.

Assignments
PA1 88/100, mean 88/100
PA2 59/100, mean 71/100
PA3 88/100, mean 84/100

Every semester, they make 1 or 2 of the PA more difficult/easier or whatever. PA2 is harder this year.

Lab
10/10

Grade I got: C

It's not bad actually, I thought I would be given an F after that finals. I don't even know how you would study for finals. For me, I spent 60% of time revising the lecture notes and going through the examples inside and 40% doing 2 past papers so don't do that.
comment_workload:
I remember in this semester spending like 2 good days trying to do that self-study lab and still not being able to complete it. The main problem with me is that I did not try hard to seek for help so

TIP: Use the piazza when you need it. Go to office hours if you are truly stuck. The first time I got into office hours, the TA was not strict about straight out giving me solution for first task of a lab.

I remember in this semester spending 5 days to do PA2, that seemingly other people writing these reviews can do within 3 days. Yet I could not finish that assignment and I got 59/100. Fact: I went to counselling because of the hopelessness I suffered while doing the PA2. Fact: When I looked at the answer to PA2. I had two thoughts:

1. What is this?
2. I am satisfied because I would never be able to think of such a complicated solution.

TIP: TO be fair, considering you start really early when the PA is given out, like 2-3 days after it's published, maybe you could finish the PA without so much pressure.

author: asssasasa
title: BIG DATA MINORS BEWAREEEE11!!111!!1
comment_content: I woke up one day thinking hmm maybe I should take a minor, something that would really help me branch out on the tech side. ok big data tech minor sounds perfect yay i was so excited to start my new journey which I knew was going to be tough since my only other coding experience was with COMP1021. So IMAGINE MY SHOCK when this is the next coding course I had to take. this course has a lovely beginning with cute little for loops ooooh while loops nice c++ syntax. fine w me. BUT TELL ME HOW we got here. they want us to be a human compiler but im literally just a girl in science doing coding for the second time EVER. THERE WAS NO IN BETWEEN. This is the type of content that takes months/years for beginners to learn and actually practice logic and retain all the information, but no they want us to fucking (sorry) coding experts by the end of 3 months. THIS IS JUST MY MINOR this is one of the most difficult languages to learn it's so unfair. from turtle graphics to fucking dynamic double linked structures with a hint of object-oriented programming. totally fucked up for big data tech minor there should be another course to just get the basics down. I don't think ust understand how learning works: ur brain needs some fucking time to process what ur doing.
comment_teaching: I did not rlly enjoy my original professor, so I watched prof dimirtris' lectures. watching the lectures was kind of a pain cuz its hard to read on the whiteboard and sometimes the projector would get cut off. but that's ok i will let that pass. i think he's a good teacher and for what its worth i quite enjoyed learning the theory.
comment_grading: hahahhaah now for the best part!!!! this course is filled with cpeg and cs and ddp kids and I'm just a big data minor <3 Id consider myself a p good student w a good gpa and i just wanted to challenge myself by doing something new. but no ust will just suck the soul out of u so don't u dare ever try doing anything outside your comfort zone! trying -> get fucking destroyed for trying <3 how did they think it was ok to pair me up w people who already have much experience with coding, and are used to using logic to solve problems (science has more memorization). when I was asking cpeg kids for help, even they were struggling with some of the PAs and mind u AGAIN I'm not supposed to get this destroyed for just a minor!!!! this course took away so much of my time, and my social life, and depleted my mental health, all for a shit grade or maybe a fail (exam in a few days). but not because i wasn't good at it or didn't try, it was literally too much way too soon. I'm a pretty fast learner and love learning but didn't get any form of support or grace from this course. super disappointed that they just didn't give a shit about people w minors and basically just put us there because there was nowhere else to go. do better.
comment_workload: it was a 4 credit course so i knew the workload was going to suck. i found the first 2 PAs manageable but the 3rd one is what threw me under the bus. i thought i figured out how to do and i passed all the initial testcases only to get thrown under the bus from the hidden ones. memory leakage was suchhh a big part of the grading and it was just super discouraging to try and then get ur soul crushed like that. and pls its not worth going through all that for what's supposed to be a minor. I was already taking on another 4 credit science course- LIFS3140 which was also one of the toughest courses for my major. was not a good combo and this comp course ruined both for me. never felt this way about any course before, always took accountability if I knew i just didnt try enough. thank u comp2011 for being my first ever ustspace review u deserve it.

author: happystar
title: Competitive and bad grade this semester
comment_content:
The content is quite useful, and the lecture note is great. With the lecture note and course materials, it is easy to self study.

However, if COMP 2011 is not your required course (e.g. QFIN). Don't choose this. Go for other COMP course. You will thank me after.

if you are CS students like me, unfortunately, this will be your destiny to stay here. Above mean is definitely not enough. Upper quartile may even give you a B+ or B. Please be at least above 1SD in order to get A-. Given all CS students requires 3.7 gpa to enter, getting above 1SD among these good students is something difficult.
comment_teaching: Cecia is nice in term of teaching. However, I think you need to pre-read the lecture note before going the lessons, or I chose to skip lessons and learn my own later after midterm.
comment_grading:
The grading is worse. I should be above average, but still B only.

PA: full mark
lab: full mark
Quiz: 19/24  mean:14 (around upper quartile)
Midterm: 61.5/100  mean: 55.5
Final: 58.5/100  mean:41.0  (my final should be around upper quartile)

ALL of my work are ABOVE MEAN. My final is even FAR ABOVE MEAN. You give me a B??????

btw, my friends get PA and lab full mark, midterm final around mean or slightly above mean. Eventually C+. Mean is C+ wtf?

For me, I understand the SD in COMP course is large. But, if a students get all the thing above mean, and except midterm all my work are around upper quartile or full mark. I think B+ is not an unreasonable demand.

Every CS students are already 3.7 or above, you even have strict curving for these good students. Why don't we just take ISOM those business programming course and get good grade????
comment_workload:
Worklaod is heavy

3 Programming Assignment (PA). Each will spend you at least 1 whole day to finish. For me, I spent 2-3 whole day for 1 PA only.

lab every week. This should be ok, just like COMP 1021 not difficult to do. Be careful and can get full mark.

This year, a newly added quiz(5%) is held on late September. So it means that you need to keep revising materials from the start to the end of the course.

author: KF8311
title: I'm psychologically traumatized!!! (我有心靈創傷啊!!!)
comment_content:
Good introduction to C++ Programming, which is completely different to COMP1021 Python, be cautious that an A+ in COMP1021 does not necessarily means you will excel in COMP2011 as Labs, PAs and exams are more challenging, you may have a hard time learning topics like Pointers, Recursion and Class/Queue/Stack etc.

Syllabus:
Introduction to computer programming
All:
C++ basics: basic syntax, data types, operators
Control flow

Final and Midterm only:
Functions
Array and structure
Recursion
Scope

Final only:
Struct
Pointers
Dynamic Data
Class
Stack and Queue
File input / output

You can check out my GitHub repository for my implementation for all PAs and Labs, might include custom test cases, I didn’t get full mark in lab 2 but I did fix it to solve the bug and all code “should” pass all test cases. They may not be the best solution but I think you can make it as a reference when doing past PAs (2425 Spring) to practice. PowerPoint slides are messy and incomplete as I rarely use them. Feel free to clone or edit them, watashi!
https://github.com/KF8311/COMP2011/
comment_teaching:
Cindy is a passionate teacher and willing to answer students' questions. The pace is average, and most people should be able to catch up.

In Lab sessions, Peter Chung teaches very good and will sing songs after his teaching, very funny

Final: (Out of 100)

Median: 45.7
Stdev: 23.92

The distribution is like two normal distribution overlapped with two peak in 40 ish and 70 ish
The final paper is not as hard you imagine but the mean is still quite low, I *heard* getting a mark of 28% in final should have a good chance of passing if your assignment part is passing score
To pass this course, it is not sufficient to have your total score greater than the overall pass mark X. You are also required to obtain a separate pass mark Y in your exams. That is, to pass, you are required to have:

(Total Score >= X) AND ((Weighted Exam Components) >= Y).

However, we promise you that X will not be greater than 50% and Y will not be greater than 30% of the respective maximum scores.

(It is assumed that all scores in the above formula are scaled to 100 maximum.)
X is usually 50% and Y will usually be (30 - a small number)%, tbh not a course for easy passing, if you perform terribly in final (let's say <20), you gg, so do not get a low mark in midterm and final if you want to pass the course.

(L1 Instructor, Brian Mak: 份巻直頭唔難啦! (Translation: The paper is not hard at all!) <- but many people fails bro, and median is like 45 ish

FINAL WARNING: be careful of year 1s they are f**king strong and will take away many A ranges, they are potential CS/DSCT contenders and will pull up the mean, UQ and high lmao. In Spring, there are more year 1s (as they finish COMP1021 and want to speedrun UST COMP/DSCT/CPEG) in the course. And who are confident to enter COMP2011? yes, the gods and the smurfers, they beat the sh*t out of ELEC year 2 for their requirement and brutally take away their survival, so uh, suggest pathway is still taking COMP2011 in year 2 fall but if you are strong, COMP2011 Spring is for you to get A ranges. Fall sem paper is usually bit easier but the cutoffs are higher as there are more CS students who senior entry'd or major select into COMP. But I mean, if you are year 1 and who are confident to get good grades in COMP2011, why not take this course and add 4 credits to your best 24 (wait no Major selection is gone after 2425). So yeah, take this course with C++ background should be a huge advantage but if you are not, work really hard for good grade.
"沒有爛Grade嘅course, 只有爛Grade嘅人" Quoted from a UST student.

For the future students who need not major selection and just get into CSE/CPEG directly, congratulations you escape from the CSE Admission test which require you to get CGA around 3.5+. Final farewell to the people enters prior to 2526, fighting really hard in COMP2011, MATH1014 or MATH1013 to earn a spot for their desired major. Next year, there might be still many year 1 in COMP2011, but they are not fighting for major entry anymore, they are just, learning C++ and prepare for their Computer Science Major. I am not a good writer, so I will conclude this with few lines of lyrics.

人皆尋夢 夢裡不分西東
片刻春風得意 未知景物矇矓

人生如夢 夢裡輾轉吉凶
尋樂不堪苦困 未識苦與樂同

天造之才 皆有其用
振翅高飛 無須在夢中

南柯長夢 夢裡不知所中
醉翁他朝醒覺 是否跨鳳成龍


天造之才 皆有其用
振翅高飛 無須在夢中

何必尋夢 夢裡甘苦皆空
勸君珍惜此際 自當欣慰無窮
何必尋夢

I will update my mark and grade after they released grade. And COMP2011 is NOT a bad course, just your peers are very strong, work hard, work really really really hard to earn a chance for yourself, or just survival. It is unavoidable for ELEC, CPEG and COMP, just try your best, seek help in Piazza and good luck!

Edit: a warm email from L1 instructor Brian Mak:
Dear students, you should be proud of yourself that you have gone through the last 13+ weeks taking COMP2011. If you are traumatized, you need to take a break and learn some relaxation skills. Do some exercise.

If you still want to (and are not forced to) take COMP2012 next year, then you were not really traumatized (what traumatized people will want another tramatization!) by COMP2011; you should have been enlightened! COMP2012 should be more fun. My experience is that students, after having taken COMP2011, are much more mature in programming, and find that COMP2012 is actually "easier". Different programming concept finally fall into places.

Take care and have fun in summary before taking COMP2012 next year.

comment_workload:
Workload is heavy and that's why it is 4 credits
For Spring semester:
1 Quiz, 1 Midterm, 1 Final
8 Labs, 3 PAs
Quiz occurs in 2nd week of semester, and it only covers first few topics

Midterm lasts for 2 hours and final is just a longer midterm with 3 hours including more topics

first few labs takes less than one hour to do but the ones with pointers takes hours to fix memory leak and heap overflow...
remember to attend the labs as they have attendance

PA1 is very easy and should take less than 3 hours
and the rest are PAIN, which lead to the title

PA2 is about "Simplified" Mathematical Expression Evaluator,
Here are the tasks you need to complete in this assignment. In chief, you are required to implement:

A set of recursive math utility functions that are used to calculate the values of mathematical functions, such as square root and power.
A set of recursive string utility functions that are used to manipulate strings, such as a parse_number function that parses a string like "3.14" to a double value 3.14.
An expression evaluator function that evaluates a mathematical expression.
An expression parenthesizer function that adds parentheses and removes extra spaces to/from the expression to make it more readable.
Task 1 and 2 are fine but task 3 and 4 are very painful as you have no idea how to achieve these task with recursions, no loops allowed! I spent my whole weekend and few days around to finish the PA, working 8 hours straight per day, INSANE. I feel my sanity jump mushroom after finishing PA2. The UGTA Harry Li makes this PA, so I will leave a song here for him.
聽我說謝謝你 因為有你 溫暖了四季
謝謝你 感謝有你 世界更美麗
我要謝謝你 因為有你 愛常在Harry
Xie Xie Ni (Thank You) Harry for making my sanity jump mushroom
Initially I want to give Workload a B but considering PA2 it should be C now, following the course title C++
I'm psychologically traumatized!!! (我有心靈創傷啊!!!)
(For your reference, this quote is used by Yahata Umiri (八幡 海鈴) in BanG Dream! Ave Mujica Episode 11, Umiri expressed her traumatization when dialing with Togawa Sakiko due to the absence of Misumi Uika and Togawa Sakiko of their band Ave Mujica training)

And PA3 is slightly better, making Coffee Shop Management System and the pointers are flying everywhere, which is quite messy and I have a hard time on it, still it is less time consuming than PA2. And for PA3 you better get a Linux Virtual environment or Linux Compiler so you can make use of address sanitizer and find memory issues in a easier way. I make use of Github Codespace which is quite useful as codespace terminal g++ is operated in a Linux environment

Usually you will use Visual Studio Code to code your assignments but you may use other editor if you want to, just no online C++ please, you will have a hard time, trust. Also cheating and copying other's code is not allowed. Using GenAI is fine but you must make it does not look so "AI" and pretend you write it yourself. It is not recommended to rely on AI as in the real exam you still need to write them with your bare hands, yes, hand written code on paper.

(Deepseek is better than copilot and GPT in terms of programming imo, still try your best to do it yourself)

PA2 and PA3 could take days or even weeks to complete, start early or else you will have no time to debug and finish them, they take up 24% of grade and need extra attention. Workload A for genius but for noobs like me it should be a C to D I guess, do not trust the AAAA reviews saying that it is 1XXX course or 3 credit workload, this is indeed a 4 CREDITS course and it is indeed a 2000 level course

Function definition, modular programming, passing parameter (PBV and PBR)

comment_teaching:
Prof Brian is a decent teacher. He obviously knows what he's teaching very well and will give you the lower level explanation of most C++ functionality if you ask for it.

The lectures are paced quite poorly in my opinion though. I dont know whether to blame him or whoever made this course syllabus but it spends way to long on basic stuff and too little time on complex topics.

comment_grading:
Ok, so my grade isn't out yet but I will edit my review once it is.

To pass this course, it is not sufficient to have your total score greater than or equal to the pass mark. You are also required to obtain a separate pass mark in your exams. That is, to pass, you are required to have
(total score >= X) AND (quiz score * 0.08 + final exam score * 0.46 >= Y).
X and Y will be determined at the end of the semester but we may promise you that:
X will not be greater than 50, and
Y will not be greater than 16.
(It is assumed that all scores in the above formula are scaled to 100 maximum.)

lab exercises:                      10%
programming assignments: 36% (10%, 12%, 14%)
quiz:                               8%
final exam:                           46%

Means:
Labs: around 2.8/3
PAs: 88.2%, 78.8%, 84.9% (acc. to Canvas)
these means were way higher than previous sems but the assignments were quite easy so I understand why.
Quiz: 79.2%
Final: 40%

Me:
Labs, PAs: 100%
Quiz: 79%
Final: 63.5%
Edit: This got me an A.
Little rant: the final was one of the most poorly designed assessment of a student's skills I have ever seen. Maybe I am just a butthurt baby but seriously, this typing code on Canvas (without being able to use Tabs or a separate text editor) is so time consuming. Plus, you have to remember all variable/class/function names otherwise you spend half your time scrolling back and forth from the code in the question and your code box. Many of the questions basically required you to write an algorithm to perform certain actions like shifting all the elements in a 2D array (without any indexing, only pointers) but you have to dry run everything in your head. Like when and where do you ever have to dry run such a complex algorithm in the real world?!??! A programming course shouldn't even have a final exam, but rather, have really hard projects instead.
comment_workload: Again, I basically knew C++ coming into this course so the weekly labs and the programming assignments were quite easy. The labs maybe took 2 hours at most, usually an hour though. The assignments took a lot longer though. People complained about memory leaks or whatever but they really aren't that big of an issue in my opinion. Use Zinc to test all your submissions and you'll probably get full too. Overall, appropriate for a 4 cr course.

author: ync
title: we grind, we sigh
comment_content:
bg: 2nd yr COMP student, getting A in COMP1021, no more previous programming experience, average IQ???.
friendly reminder:
1.If this course is not needed to fulfil your requirements, I generally don’t recommend taking it unless you achieve A range in COMP1021.
2. If you are a yr1 student and aiming to get into CSE, consider your own situation carefully before enrolling in this course, so that you won’t jeopardize your semester and cga.(I know that some of you are geniuses and can easily handle the stuff)

Now let's go back to the content.
Week 1-3:
Basics, Control Flow. Very basic knowledge about C++ programming, but still lots of details to notice if you only did programming on Python previously.
Week 4-6 (before midterm):
Function (Basics and Recursion), Array. The 2 most difficult topics before the midterm are recursion and array. It’s not easy to think recursively as our brain isn’t designed to think that way. Array have many rules to follow, and it’s very easy to make mistakes like segmentation fault.
Week 7-10:
Scope, Struct, Function (overloading, declaration, default arguments). This part is quite decent compared to others, and it covers some knowledge that you need to know before learning class, pointer.
Week 11-14:
Pointer, Class, Stack & Queue. This is the most challenging part of this course. Lots of concepts and rules are introduced in this part. Personally, I hate the combination of dynamic memory, string and class the most.(Actually I had a similar question about that on the final and messed it up - losing half of the marks on that 18 pts LQ)
comment_teaching:
lecture:
Dimitris’ teaching is decent. He speaks English with a distinct accent, making ‘r’ sound very special, and his tone and talking speed are a bit different from typical English. But I can understand most of that after two or three lectures. Anyway, He is very passionate about teaching and he would like to make interactions with students, fostering a lively classroom environment. After the lectures, he is also friendly to answer questions.

lab:
We have a lab every week except the midterm week, and the lab is actually a half-an-hour tutorial. During the lab, contents that are helpful for programming but not tested in exams like debug and separate compilation are taught by TA. In our section, the TA is Peter. Peter is an interesting guy who likes making political jokes. His teaching is ok but a bit rushed, leaving no time for us to try on our own. In every lecture, Peter uses some memes about our people’s GREAT GREAT Leader Xi (and sometimes Kim) to make us feel engaged, demonstrating that HK is a place embracing free-speech.
comment_grading:
lab(12%):
Each lab is counted as 3 marks:
1 mark for attendance, recorded before lab session;
1 mark for lab assignment, which is like a homework available 1 week before lab session and due after the lab session;
1 mark for Q&A session, where students are selected to answer a short question about lab assignment.(once every 2 weeks, if the student is not selected, the 1 mark will be counted base on lab assignment)
The total 12% is given according to the percentage of your lab mark.

PA(24%):
Each PA is worth 8%. Before the ddl, about one-third of the test cases are provided for testing, and others are hidden test cases. The PAs are easier compared to previous semesters, with the mean of each being nearly 90.

Exams: hand-written exam with short questions and long questions. In short questions, the format is usually like, providing a program and asking for the output, or filling in the blanks of a piece of code. Every question requires significant effort and can be very annoying. For long questions, the format is like writing a function so that it can work as expected. Since you cannot use a computer, every minor mistake or carelessness during the thinking process will get you into trouble, and you cannot vertify it by running on the computer.

Midterm(24%): mean-37/70 sd-12
The midterm is 2-hr long, testing the content during week 1-6. There are around 10 questions in total, with 6-7 short questions and 3-4 long questions. The most challenging questions of midterm are an SQ about reference variable, a LQ about recursive function, 2 LQ about array.
Final(40%): mean-51/100 sd-23
The final is 3-hr long, testing the content throughout this semester. There are around 10 questions in total, with 6-7 short questions and 3-4 long questions. The most challenging questions of the final are an SQ about scope, pointer, and array, an SQ about loop, a LQ about recursive function, 2 LQ about dynamic array.

B is given to students getting mean of the exams.
A range is 1SD or above.(it seems like I’m near the cutoff of A range, with total 81.5%)
/*update: final grade is A-, I guess I'm slightly above the cutoff, maybe few pts. one of my frds got b+ with 77.5%*/
To pass this course, students need to have total score >= X%, weighted exam score >= Y%, where X is below 50, and Y is below 30.
comment_workload:
1 lab per week + 3 programming assignments
The workload is even huge compared to other 4-cre courses. Each lab typically took me 2 hours to finish, except the one about recursion which was very annoying to understand the logic. For the PAs, the first two took me 3 days, around 8 hours each to finish. The last one, I spent a week, around 12 hours to finish, including a day purely for debugging (why are there so many memory leaks and segmentation faults…?). Also, since the difficulty of the content, I also spent 1-2 hours after each lecture to revise.

author: mmmmmmmmmm
title: IF YOU'RE YEAR 1 READ THIS
comment_content: good but archaic, focuses on logical thinking and simple code to solve puzzles instead of practical coding
comment_teaching: good, just refer to other comments i dont go to classes often but i have only heard good things about them
comment_grading:
I got a B+, but that really isn't important.

POV: You're Year 1 looking to get into CS, just got past your fall semester probably with an A range in 1021/1022P, found it easy and/or fun and want to take more coding courses. Now, if it wasn't for the fact that you need GPA to actually choose your major, you probably would've already decided to take 2011, and I would fully support that decision. But the matter of fact is, you still need to keep your GPA high which is exactly why I discourage you from taking this course unless you have a very good reason to. Basically all of the engineering introductory courses are way easier than this course even if you like programming over those subjects. If I had the chance to choose again, I would just wait until Year 2 to take this course and just pre-enrolled in something like CIVL1100 instead, even though I didn't plan on choosing CIVL as my major. Hell, I'd even rather just have one less course in my semester to focus on my other subjects and get a better grade on those instead. In my opinion, its almost never worth it.

If you're still determined to take this course and are like "Nah, I'd win", then I salute you and wish you good luck. Put in consistent effort, its not like 1021 where you can go all in on your exam. I got below mean for my midterm as a result of winging it and not being used to having to write code on paper instead on a computer, and thats basically the end of my hopes of getting an A range even though I didn't realize it at the time. I tried to fix it and did well in my finals (82), but that wasn't enough to get me an A range which was unfortunate. Anyways that's enough of an old dog's rambling. Just know, even though you are smart, so is everyone else in the course. The only edge you can have over them is putting in more time and effort than them consistently.
comment_workload: Its not light. That's all I can say. Personally, I like coding, I find coding fun, which is why I took 2011 in Year 1 even though I knew it wasn't going to be easy. But listen, if you really like coding so much, you literally have 3 years later to do it, but there are other things which if you don't do now, it will be too late. Make new friends. Join some fun events. Your U-life in UST is already over after Year 1 so enjoy it while it lasts.

author: Haydennnnn
title: Hard work earns me an A+
comment_content:
The content is great. C++ is fun. However, at first it might be confusing to people who used to program in Python (like me). In Python, the syntax is very casual. But in C++, it is very strict.

Many people think "pointers" is the hardest topic. I agree, but I think the hardest thing to fully master is "at what circumstances there is an error".

In exams, they can give you a VERY STRANGE CODE (I bet no one will ever code like that) and then ask you whether there is a compilation error / runtime error. I HATE THESE QUESTIONS THE MOST. I will never understand why I need to know whether there is an error when I never code like that. It is like asking a Chinese person about Spanish grammar.
comment_teaching: Cecia teaches very well. Very clear, very engaging.
comment_grading:
Midterm and Final exam. I got 99% in the midterm and 95.25% in the final. A+ as expected.

The highest score in the midterm and the final was 99% (that was me) and 95.7% (that was my friend). The good thing here is that the marks are divided into many fragments. Even if you are careless about something, you only lose 1 or 2 marks at most. (In contrast, in COMP1021, mistyping one letter can cost you 8 marks --- I experienced it.)

I think I also got the highest overall score in the course. (Yes, I'm showing off)
My overall score is 97.83%, while the highest score shown in Canvas is 97.8%. It looks like Canvas is correcting the high score to 1 decimal place. So I am not the highest if someone got 97.84%.
comment_workload:
8 lab assignments, 3 programming assignments (PAs).

In my opinion, all (except the last programming assignment) were easy and can be finished within hours.

The last programming assignments took me exactly 2 whole days (A whole Saturday and a whole Sunday) to complete. Including debugging, which took me 6-7 hours.

I heard other students complain about the workload of the last programming assignment. The professor replied that typically, 2 days aren't enough for the last assignment.

Since there are hidden testcases, you never know whether your code is really correct, even if you can pass all the shown testcases. Therefore, you really have to be VERY careful when you code.

Some of my friends passed all the shown testcases on the first attempt. But they aren't lucky enough to pass the remaining ones. I, on the other hand, was lucky? Or unlucky? Depending on how you think about it. It's somewhat like 賽翁之馬.

I was unlucky that for PA3, I failed almost all the testcases. And I need to debug case by case (each debugging only solves one or two testcase). And that's why that took me 7 hours. However, after all these debuggings, I was so lucky that I passed all the hidden testcases. I got 100% for all labs and PAs.

author: kafka!!
title: Learnt a lot, but if I could turn back time, I wouldn't have taken it this early
comment_content:
Refer to other posts for the syllabus. Theres a lot of content to be covered.

Generally, the hardest content before the midterm for me was recursion. After the midterm, really really focus on pointers, and dynamic memory. But honestly, how I view it is:

Before the midterm, all the topics are kind of within their own bubble and can be understood separately. But the content after the midterm is very very inter-related. Things like function overloading aren't standalone topics because they teach you how constructors of classes can be overloaded later on. The entire topic of dynamic memory and pointers has so many other applications as well, etc.

My advice is that before the midterm, you can focus on each topic individually, and you'll be fine just practicing a couple past papers and COMP2011 self tests (on the course website>resources).

However for the topics after the midterm, I think you should really try to see the bigger picture of what you're learning. Especially if u plan on proceeding to 2012.



One of the key tips to understand with cpp is that you really need to understand how things work under the hood. Its not like python where you dont really need to think about how the computer program REALLY interacts with your computer.

For example, in python, you wouldn't ever see a statement like 1+'a', because how does that make sense right? how can you add a to 1? But in cpp we learn how these characters are all binary numbers that have equivalent integer values, so here the character 'a' can be turned into an integer and be added to 1.

Memory allocation, in particular, is key to being able to do well in 2011. In python, you dont ever have to explicitly allocate memory spaces for anything. In fact, we dont even really consider how the program interacts with memory at all. But CPP lacks a lot of the conveniences of languages like python, so u will need to learn DEEPLY how memory works for different variables and data structures.

author: hime
title: This review is written from perspective of a non-cse student who has little programming knowledge beforehand
comment_content:
DISCLAIMER: If you are mature in C++ programming before taking this course, this review is not suitable for you as you should be able to master this course easily.

I am a non-CSE student who has little programming background. I took this course for interest and you can expect there are quite a number of students who already have learnt all the content before taking this course.

For the syllabus in details, please see the other recent reviews as I believe they do not change it. Basically it covers all the basics of c++ programming, i.e., array, string, recursion, pointer, class, etc.

It is not hard in the beginning as it is quite straight forward and your should cover those ideas in COMP1XXX. Things start to become harder when it covers recursive function, and the difficulties comes to its maximum at pointer.
comment_teaching: Cecia is a passionate lecturer, I can feel that she always tries her best to make everything clear. However, her tone is kind of dull (maybe due to zoom lecture instead of face-to-face) so you may fall asleep in the middle. Overall she is still a good lecturer.
comment_grading:
Overall Weighting
Lab 10%
Assignment 8, 10, 14, 12%
Final Exam 44%
Trial Run of Final Exam 1%

As I applied P/F for this semester, I don't really care too much about the grading, yet you still need to get a 50% overall AND 35% (35/100) in your final exam in order to pass this course. As far as I know, this course is very competitive so if you really want to get a good grade, you have to get almost full mark in all the assignments as well as exams.
comment_workload:
In my opinion, the workload of this course is actually normal as a 4-credit course. There is a lab released in every week. However there is no plagiarism checking for labs so you can simply copy the code from your friends or even from online, yet doing it by yourself is still highly recommended.

The programming assignments(PA) is the main workload during the semester, they are significantly more complex than the labs, so you probably need more time to finish them. In general, I highly recommend to start working on it once it is released because I don't think you can use one day to finish the whole PA, especially for the later ones. The first two PAs are not that hard so most people get full marks in them. However, PA3 is really disastrous and mind-blowing, you probably need to spend a lot of time to understand the requirement even you start to code anything for it. PA4 is less difficult than PA3, but it is still hard. Generally speaking , the workload of the PAs is quite high, especially for the last two PAs, you probably need to spend a lot of time in grinding it if you really want to get full mark in all PAs.

P.S. Don't try to copy directly from others for PA, it definitely doesn't work in the way you want.

author: Stephen Chang
title: Pretty easy for students who've done coding or taken coding classes
comment_content:
The following are the main topics covered in this course:
1- C++ Basics (intro to coding, c++ syntax, types, variables, etc)
2- Control Flow (if statements, while and for loops)
3- Functions (pass-by-value/reference, Recursion, etc)
4- Array (1D and 2D, C-string)
5- Scope
6- Struct
7- Pointer (spent the most time on this topic, l/rvalue, address, "pass-by-pointer", dynamic memory, linked lists, etc)
8- Class
9- Stacks and Queues

Overall, I would consider these topics to be easy and quite basic, as I've already learned about many of them.

Some topics that are more confusing, especially if you have less coding experience, are Recursions and Pointers.

With recursions you have to change the way you think about problems, so I'd suggest spending a bit more time on this topic and do some practice problems and code to get familiar with it. I have learned about recursions before, so I did not struggle much on it.

Pointer is a new concept for me, so I had to spend a bit more effort understanding it, but overall I think it's very simple conceptually. The difficult part is being careful with unusual cases, such as const pointer vs pointer to const and pointers to pointers, and all the cases with linked lists such as the head and tail.

Due to the nature of coding, I'd highly recommend messing around with code on your own. The lecture slides provide lots of example code that you can copy and experiment on. It's useful in helping you build up an instinctive understanding of the C++ syntax and to think like a computer.
comment_teaching:
Dimitris Papadopoulous is a great teacher. I love the way he teaches, especially with how he encourages us to ask questions. He is very engaging and fun to listen to. He also asks good questions that are not on the lecture material that get my head scratching and help me understand the material deeper.

The lecture slides are well made and contain everything you need to know for the course (plus some optional extra info). You do not need to attend lectures to succeed in this course, but in my case I liked Dimitris's teaching so I joined most of them. Like I mentioned in the content section, there are also lots of example code that I suggest you copy and practice with.

Overall, the course content is structured clearly and helpful in understanding. You can do well on the exams just by studying the lecture slides and past-papers.

A+ is, of course, an amazing grade for me. Based on other people's scores, I believe I barely passed the line between A and A+. Make sure to be careful with labs and PAs, these are assignments that you should easily get a high score on. If I was a bit lazy and got a slightly lower score on any of them, I would've gone from A+ to A.
comment_workload:
I have previously learned Java and self learned Python, so I was able to understand most of the course's topic without spending any time studying. I also spent relatively little time studying for the exams, instead choosing to focus on other classes. For those with less coding experience, your workload may be greater in this aspect.

Labs are super easy, less than 5 min for the first 2 labs, and less than 30 min for the rest.
PAs may take more time. For me, it took like 1-2 hours just to read and fully understand the assignment scenario and what it wants me to do. The actual coding is not too difficult once you understand it, though it may still take quite a bit of time depending on how many tasks are in the PA. The last PA took me a lot of time, maybe like 7+ hours or something like that.

Like I mentioned in the grading section, be careful on the labs and PAs. Aside from hidden test cases, you're allowed to check your score before deadline. As such, you should aim to get 100% on the public test cases. If you're short on time, feel free to use AI to double check your code. Do not use AI to do your assignments, it is not worth it. If, even with ample time, you're unable to do the labs or PAs without AI, you're most likely gonna struggle on the exams since they contain questions that are similar in style to the labs and PAs (though not as long or complicated as the PAs ofc).

comment_content:

5. Recursion
6. Scope
7. Structs
8. Function Overloading and Signature
9. Pointers
10. C++ Classes
11. Stacks & Queues

All topics are easy in this course. If I had to choose the most challenging, I would choose Recursion, which is quite thought-provoking in exams. You need to know clearly what the cases are and what the logical sequences are.

Pointers look difficult but are generally annoying because you need to consider different cases: inserting at the head, inserting in the middle, and inserting at the tail, and also handling the nullptr case for specific items.

The midterm and final exams are simple and similar to past papers.
Study well, understand the material, and you'll be good.

author: zsy
title: Good class if you want to avoid COMP1021
comment_content:
Most contents are already there in many other reviews. This is copied from others:

Part 1: Basics and Controls
Part 2: Function (Introduction)
Part 3: Array
Part 4: Recursion

Part 5: Scope
Part 6: Function III:
  - Function declaration
  - Function overloading
  - Default Function Argument

Part 7: Separate Compilation
Part 8: C++ Structure

Part 9: Pointers (Intro)
  - lvalue & rvalue
  - dereference a pointer
  - "const" in pointers initialization
  - Array as (constant) pointers

Part 10: Pointers (Usage)
  - Dynamic allocations (on Heap)
  - Problems on dynamic objects:
     ! Dangling pointer
     ! Memory leak
  - Linked List (LL)
  - LL + Binary Tree
  - Dynamic 1D and 2D array

Part 11: C++ Class (Intro)
  - Class == Struct with its own functions
  - keywords "public" and "private"
  - Constructors
     Trigger when a new object is created
  - Destructor
     Need when the object has pointers
  - Accessor
     Always followed by "const"
     Should NOT modify its data members
  - Mutator
     Opposite to accessors
  - Scope operator
     Syntax: <class_name>::
     Use to separate global function and
     member functions (Class scope)

Part 12: Stack and Queue
  - Stack: Last In First Out (LIFO)
  - Queue: First In First Out (FIFO)
  - Stack & Queue + Class

3. Final: Though I got a shitty grade but I really did try HARD for exam prep...remember to finish all the past papers and be familiar with all the ppt contents! comment_content:
Nearly all the C++ Basic, but some important but harder ones are left to COMP2012.

The coverage of topics:
Topics:
1.  C++ Basics
2. Control Flow
3. Basics 2
4. Control 2
5. Function 1
6. Array
7. Function 2 Recursion
8. Scope
9. Struct
10. Function 3 Miscellaneous
11. Separate Compilation
12. Pointer
13. C++ Class
14. Stack and Queue

Intended learning outcomes:
1. Use common software tools to develop and debug a program written in an OOP language. : Use an IDE such as VS Code proficiently to write, compile, run, and debug a C++ program consisting of one or many source files.
2. Write a short program to solve a simple problem in an OOP language.: Construct a solution to a written problem by writing a complete C++ program of no more than 500 lines of codes on one's own. !without the requirements are clearly explained and the required programming constructs are told!
3. Demonstrate that recursive and non-recursive functions are abstractions of sub-problems in a task.: Demonstrate thorough understanding of how recursion works. Be able to develop a recursive solution to a written problem on one's own, and sometimes contrast it with the corresponding non-recursive solution.
4. Describe the concept and the use of pointers in indirect addressing and dynamic memory allocation.: Demonstrate strong understanding of the concept of pointers. Is able to use pointers effectively in indirect addressing and dynamic memory allocation in a great variety of scenarios.
5. Demonstrate the use of several data structures.:Is able to choose the appropriate data structures such as linked lists, stacks and queues to solve problems, and implement the solution in C++ on one's own.
6. Implement an abstract data type by defining a class in an OOP language.: Given the description of a simple ADT, is able to implement it with a complete C++ class definition that includes appropriate class members and class member functions. Is able to write programs that create and manipulate ADT objects. (ADT: abstract data type)

comment_grading:

If you happen to get a mean, unfortunately many C+ in C++ class... You will probably score a C+... That is unlike to the school business school norm. Usually if you are close to/ at mean, then you can gain a B-/ B. But it seems this is another story for engineering course...

However, with a class size of 400, there was quite a number of genius students who did not only did pretty good job on nailing the course but also helped the other students on piazza.
I really appreciate them! I can see the tech talents are really rising... Thanks all who helped me on the learning journey despite the online mode.
comment_workload:
*Super heavy but worthy if you are pursuing a software engineering career*

From the perspective of a business student, the workload is super heavy... As a 4 credit course, it should be heavy, but the actual amount of work is even beyond that... Every week, apart from lecture hours, it is the best to spare 7-8 hours to revise and finish the labs. For the assignments, it is the best to spare 24 hours (accumulated) for each one if you aim to finish them near perfection. I counted my hours that I spent on this course, the hour was crazy (maybe it is because I don't really adapt to the mode of a programmer which is aka sitting in front of the computer for an afternoon/ a whole day straight every weekend.)

author: DrainGang
title: Have fun fighting cheaters
comment_content:
Basic C++ content:
C++ basics: basic syntax, data types, operators
Control flow
Functions
Array and structure
Recursion
Scope
Struct
Pointers

File input / output (not in exam)

Necessary for CS/CPEG students, and therefore as a CPEG student it was useful and I would say that it is quite interesting if you are interested in programming. If you are a year 1 student I would not suggest this course however unless you already know C++ well or VERY confident in your abilities.
comment_teaching:
Rated at B, but is more of a B-.

It is commendable that Cecia does try to make the lectures interesting with some live coding sections, but in the end she ends up mostly repeating what is written on the lecture powerpoints.

Speaking of the powerpoints, they were only acceptable for learning the most basic of the concepts, if you think these can help you do the labs or the assignments, you would be wrong. Spend time learning on Youtube/Google instead. Lectures were not mandatory so its up to you if you want to attend.

Labs were OK, wait for your turn to demonstrate the code and you are done, was arranged a bit messily at first but got OK later. You could demo the code before it is your turn if you just join the Zoom meeting before the lab session.
comment_grading:
Lab: 10%
Programming assignments 1: 10%
Programming assignments 2: 12%
Programming assignments 3: 16%
Quiz: 8%
Final exam: 46%

Lab: 1 per week, finish a program related to the course content that week, not to difficult to get full marks since the programs are not too complex, and you can always "ask" for help.

Quiz: Taken on a month into the semester, short refresher for things like if-else, loops and such. Averages were high. (mean: 18/24)

Programming Assignments: 3 in total, each were on a different area (recursion, pointers and such), challenging, takes a few days, however, cheating was rampant with the assignments, so averages for them were absurdly high and the department has done nothing to combat people buying code off of others, so prepared to lose a few percent of the final score if you either have integrity or are not highly skilled in C++.

Finals: 45 minute sessions, 3 sessions in total, all short questions based on reading code/writing code, this meant studying the notes were almost completely useless. This also means that if you have spent time writing code you would probably do slightly better. The exam was also formatted poorly, meaning you have to scroll up and down just to even read the question, and you have to type code in the CANVAS message box, which gives you no option to use tab to space out your code, and no other formatting options.

However none of these really mattered since the final was outrageously difficult. You are expected to finish 2 programs (about 8 functions in total) in about 35 minutes, which is absurd, mean at the end was about 40/100.

My grades:
Labs: full mark
Assignments: 9/10%, 9.5/12%, 16/16%
Quiz: 20/24
Finals: 45/100

Over all rounds up to about mean or so, and got a B, instead of a B- that I was expected, therefore grading is good in my opinion.
comment_workload:
Quite heavy.

Each lab takes a few hours to complete, especially the later ones as the later labs requires you to finish 4-5 different functions, and the difficulty gap between the lecture notes and the labs/assignments is huge, so you need to spend time learning everything yourself. Assignments usually take a few days to complete, if you are really stuck there could be a chance that someone on the piazza (discussion forum for the course) will help you.

However at the same time because you need to do so much studying just to finish the lab and assignments, if you actually did everything yourself (Hard to say for people in this course!), you would have a decent idea of everything, especially for writing code which is VERY important for the final as I have explained.

comment_grading:
The grades are distributed as below.
1. Quiz (5%)
2. Mid term ( 25%)
3. Assignment ( 24 %)
4. Lab ( 10%)
5. Final (36%)

The quiz is during the beginning of the semester and quite easy if you have some back ground as not much has been taught.

comment_workload: The work load at the beginning of the semester is quite low but it increases exponentially. The labs get harder as you reach towards the end. author: matthew12zz
comment_content:
Basic C++ like syntax, functions, ect.
Hard parts are within OOP:

- global and local variables(very tricky in keeping track)
- pointers and linked lists
Really not much, key is to do additional exercises to reinforce understanding.
comment_teaching:

comment_grading:

Exams are very hard with averages in 50-60s.

A+

Professor gives great support to solve problem, but it doesn't need to take attendance in the Lecture session, so you can skip the lecture.

Actually, you can study by yourself, if you have the C++ coding background, that means you can skip the all of the lectures and just review the slides.But if you skip the lecture, you will also skip some details never occurred in the slides, but will occur in the exam, e.g. the difference between the i++ and ++I, could we use a++++ or ++++a(use ++++a is valid), or the difference between the const int *a and int const *a.

Total. 100%

author: Normancium
comment_content:
The course format includes lectures and labs/tutorials. Attendance is not required for lectures but is required for the labs/tutorials. Attendance for the labs is very strict and accounts for large portion of that lab's score. There is one quiz, one midterm, and one final exam. There are a total of around seven labs, but this depends on the time available. Each lab requires a few hours to complete and includes instructions. Also, there are three programming assignments, which may take up to a few days.

The TA's were very helpful. Even if you had difficulties while doing the assignments, they would still give you hints. They aren't allowed to directly help you solve the problems, but their hints are more than enough for you to figure it out yourself.

The syllabus includes the basics of C++ programming (e.g., syntax, the concept of compilation), control, functions, scope, pointers, recursion, stacks, queues, structures, classes and arrays. There are also self-study topics which include file input and output, separate compilation and the string class.

The first half of the course was not too bad. When they get to topic on pointers, stuff gets pretty difficult. But the instructors give plenty of past papers, which I found to be very helpful.

Note: although the materials may seem okay, the problems in the exam are much harder.

Note: although it was said that the self-study topics will not be assessed, they are still somewhat included in the exams.
comment_teaching: This instructor's explanations were very clear. Although our section was relatively slow compared to the others, the pace was just right. The instructor encouraged students to ask questions, which was good. For harder topics such as linked lists, she also made her own slides with her own examples which I found very helpful. She is also very responsive to emails and would give thorough explanations.
comment_grading:
The mean was around 60 to 70 for the midterm and final exams. The mean was pretty high for the quiz.

The programming assignments (PAs) and labs contributed a significant part to your final grade. The weighting of the midterm and final exams were pretty similar.
comment_workload: For a 4-credit course, the large workload is understandable. The labs were almost weekly. There were no readings required, but the suggested textbook was very helpful.

Topics: C++ Basic and Control, Function and Recursion, Array, Scope, Struct, Pointers and Dynamic Data, Class, Stack and Queue.

Topics Under Pointers and Dynamic Data:
-Addressing, heap
-pointer to constant objects, pointer to pointer, pointer vs reference
-memory leak and dangling pointer
-linked list, array and pointer, and indirect addressing

Most of the content covered will overlap with prerequisite courses, but now do them in C++, except for pointers and classes (OOP). Classes won't be taught very profoundly as it is just a small intro to the next course, COMP2012. The main difficulty will be pointers and dynamic data; you can't escape them as they will appear in the programming assessment and/or final.
comment_teaching: PAPADOPOULOS's teaching is clear and passionate. The professor does have an accent, but it hasn't affected my understanding much.
comment_grading:
The weekly lab (10%) is short and simple, only to encourage students to try implementing what they learn in lectures.

There are three programming Assignments (24%), where the majority will get full marks. Make sure to check boundary cases for arrays and loops, and memory leaks for pointers.

For mid-term (26%) and final (40%), it is more of a reading comprehension, where speed is essential. Also, lab and assessment may be simplified and put into the exam as a question. Make sure to attend the paper check session.

I sit slightly above the mean for assignments, Midterms and exams, and my overall grade is B.
comment_workload:
The heaviness of the workload for a course varies from person to person, it might not apply to you. From my point of view, the workload is not heavy for a four-credit course.

First of all, the 2 hours online labs are one-by-one questioning sessions about your weekly lab code, which a TA will ask about how your code works. It doesn't last that long; you can leave after answering the questions or leave immediately if you're not picked for answering questions.

For programming assignments, a skeleton code will be provided, and you are only required to implement certain functions, which is only around 10 to 30 lines long for each function.

The difficulty comes in programming assignments PA. You need to fully understand what the code is doing, what you need to do, what given variables mean, what special weird boundary cases you need to consider, and probably more. Testcases given by the PA maker is not sufficient and you need to make your own testcases to test your code. (I fked up PA3 because the given testcases are freaking useless) Don't try to skip the memory leak testing. Zinc can detect your memory leak.
Mid-term and final exams are very unusual. You need to write your code on paper. (Who does that in real life) For me, I am fine at doing that, but I have heard complaints that it makes the exams harder. The length of the question is similar to COMP 1021. author: Dade
comment_content:
This comprehensive C++ course delves into the fundamental concepts of programming using the C++ language. Starting with the basics, learners will gain a solid understanding of variables, data types, control structures, and functions. As the course progresses, it ventures into more advanced topics such as object-oriented programming (OOP) principles, classes, and inheritance.

One noteworthy aspect of this course is the in-depth exploration of pointers, a concept that can be challenging even for those with prior knowledge of C++. The module on pointers thoroughly covers their usage, syntax, and the intricate details involved in memory manipulation. Students will be guided through practical examples and exercises to master this critical yet complex aspect of C++ programming.

Throughout the course, emphasis is placed on hands-on coding exercises, ensuring that participants not only grasp theoretical concepts but also develop practical coding skills. By the end of the program, students will have a comprehensive understanding of C++ and will have successfully navigated both the foundational and more intricate chapters, including the challenging topic of pointers.

author: Uras
comment_content: I believe the course adequately teaches a lot of basic programming skills and gives you a decent understanding of beginner level C++. For someone who has started out with python, a lot of the course contents will be very new and hard to grasp. Although the course mostly tests your coding ability and not your C++ knowledge, integrating these new concepts can be quite difficult. Some topics such as functions or recursion will be familiar, but they are already a big step up from what you will learn in first year courses. So, when topics such as pointers that don't commonly exist in most other languages come into play, most people have a really hard time wrapping their heads around them. Therefore I suggest not expecting a high grade from this course if you don't have any prior experience (besides introductory python course) OR you don't intend to invest most of your time into it. As a side note, if you are intending to take this course in the future, I highly recommend taking Java first.
comment_teaching: Although Professor Brian Mak has a nice way of teaching concepts quite well, I found his pace to be very slow. This was evident by the fact that we were many lectures behind all other sections.  His speech and his tone of voice are too calm and slow which personally makes it hard to concentrate for more than 10 minutes. But besides his pacing, he is actually very interactive with the class and explains concepts nicely, although sometimes he explains the same thing too many times.
comment_grading:
The grading is heavily weighed towards the exams. You can expect most people to get full marks on the labs, which in my case left only the assignments and the exams. Assignments were 8% each, meaning as long as you get 90+ it shouldn't affect your grade too much. Here are my grades for reference:

Assignments (Average): 95~/100
Labs: 3/3
Midterm: 91.5/100
Final: 80.25/100
Grade: A+
comment_workload:
The workload is quite light overall but very compactly distributed. There are 3 long Programming Assignments, but you are given 2 weeks to complete each of them. It might sound like a long time, but with the debugging it can take several days to complete which can be hard when combined with other courses. The assignments themselves were (for me) hard to get full marks (but not hard to get good marks) on because they are quite expansive and it is hard to get past every hidden test case (i.e. your code isn't smart enough to fit every scenario).

There are also labs every week which take around 5-10 minutes to complete. Free marks.

comment_grading:
10% Lab exercises
10% Quiz
37% programming assignments
43% Final exam
0% attendance

Lab x 10:
Each week has one lab, force us to practice the topic learned that week. You would have to demonstrate your work to the TAs in zoom and answer one question from the TAs in order to score full marks for each lab. The questions are usually straight forward and easy to answer so no worry. Try your best to do it by yourself, or else you need to pick up a lot of stuff to prepare for the exam.

Quiz:
35 MC questions (mean: 20.47/35)
Take place in the first few weeks. Testing the basic syntax and functions (e.g. if-else, for loops, data types ...). A lot of tricky questions. Rmb to have a good time management cause time is definitely not enough to finish the whole quiz.

Programming assignments:
Have ~20 days to finish one assignment. Suggestion: Start the assignment at least one week before the deadline. Most of the tasks are not difficult in terms of coding but takes time to sort out the logic.

Final exam:
3 parts, each part has 1 short question that asks students to find the output of a given program. Follow by a long coding question. Suggestions: Do pastpapers. You can find some pattern by looking at the answers of the pastpapers. The questions are usually 2D dynamic array, circular linked list, binominal tree, class .... Memorize the patterns/coding and you can at least get some scores even though you might not know what the question is really asking.
comment_workload:
HEAVY Workload
Lab work each week + programming assignment each month

- For later topics like struct and pointers, the lab and assignment are very time consuming for me

author: Dicaprio
title: More Challenging than expected but still good
comment_content:
https://course.cse.ust.hk/comp2011
https://course.cse.ust.hk/comp2011_2021F
https://course.cse.ust.hk/comp2011_2021S
actually, looking at the course website directly yourself maybe better than reading other people's comments. (need account to log in but you should have registered one in comp 1021)(some past pages may no longer be available but you can change the year and term and find it yourself)

lab sessions need attendance. they randomize the order every time to check your lab assignment. Each TA check ~30 students and there are 2~3 TA (different sessions different number) if you are lucky to be the first one, you just need to stay for 15 minutes in the lab session. if you are the last one, you need to wait for 75 minutes in the lab session. if you fail to complete your lab, you need the whole 120 minutes.

I learned C++ for HKDSE ICT Programing. I got 5 in the end and this course is still a bit challenging for me.
Things not taught in secondary school/COMP 1021:
Separate Compilation (using multiple cpp files in one program)
Pointers (a type of variable that saves the address of another variable)
Struct (a user defined data type)
Class (a more complicated Struct with functions)
Stack and queue (they uses pointers to implement stack and queue and linked list)
comment_teaching:
Cindy is a really good teacher. She explained the concepts very clearly and will answer your questions. I attended all lectures even though I enrolled in 9am lectures 🙃

The teaching team also uses Piazza, an online platform for us to ask questions. The professors/TAs/other students will answer your questions very quickly. it encourages someone as shy as me to ask questions so the whole teaching team is good.
comment_grading:
10 labs (10%)
Programming Assignment 1 (10%)
Programming Assignment 2 (12%)
Programming Assignment 3 (15%)
Mid-term quiz (10%)
Final exam (43%)

the mid-term quiz is quite early compare to other courses. (early March, ~week 4/5)
comment_workload:
10 labs, 3 assignments in total so there must be a lab/assignment every week.

Labs are simple c++ projects that will be checked during lab sessions. It can be finished in 2~3 hours.

the 3 programming assignments are a much bigger c++ projects. (they count more than mid-term) You will need to upload to ZINC, an online automatic grader to grade your code. You have unlimited trials but ZINC is quite busy near deadline (wait 30 min for grading report) There are many hidden cases so you might not get full mark if you don't check your code carefully. You may need 2~3 days to finish the assignment.

comment_workload:
3 Personal Assignment
The Personal Assignments are time-consuming, it cost weeks to do the debugging, and every single error could be disaster, especially the hidden test case are very tough to deal with. Sometimes, even the TA may not get the appropriate answer at first time

10 Labs
The Labs are quite easy, usually finished each within 2 hours if you don't want to try the extra part. But it may cost days for those who are not familiar with.

1 MC Quiz
The MC Quiz is quite tricky, and we cannot modify the answer of MC during the quiz(Online Quiz in Canvas), which is pretty odd, it is easily to lose marks in that part, but lucky it only count 10%, you still can catch up if you lose too some in this part.

1 Final
The Final is fine, as many questions are come from ideas of labs, assignments and the past papers, it is recommended to revisit those parts. It would be easy to go if you are familiar with those topic very much

Overall, I think the wordload is fair if you are very interested in programming or have fundamental background of programming in C/C++, otherwise you may get into trouble while dealing with recursion, pointer and linked list as well.

PA3: me: 100/100 (mean= 73.37, sd= 29.4, 187 students with 90-100)
Final: me: 33/100 (mean= 40.28, sd= 21.32, 205 students above mean)
Total:  around 65/100
Letter Grade: B-

I am not a good quick thinker, not very experienced in programming and not fully prepared for the final exam, so I did relatively poorly in exam and quiz.

Was expecting C+, but got 1 higher subgrade, I am happy about that already, as this course is hyper competitive.
comment_workload:
I guess it's reasonable for a 4 credit course. If it was a 3 cred course, I would give an E.

Nearly every week, you need to hand in a lab homework which cost you a few hours per week. And throughout the semester, you got to do 3 Programming assignments (PAs), each of which gives you about 2 weeks to work on. And then of course you have a quiz/mid-term, and a final exam. Because of COVID, the tests were all done on Canvas.

I would argue this course should be called as "Introduction to OOP and Algorithm" instead, because you always need to think of different algorithms to make your program work in labs and PAs. As someone who has nearly 0 programming background (I only took VBA 1022q before), thinking algorithms on a whim is challenging to me. It would be ideal if you can find someone to work and brainstorm together, because that's what I did to secure my PA scores.

Again, as someone who has very little programing background (I am a SBM student), the concept of recursion overwhelmed me a bit when I first learned about it, but later picked it up with the help of friendship. Surprisingly, I don't find pointer really hard, it is just confusing sometimes when you assign a lot of pointers, such as a binary tree structure, so make sure you have a crystal clear flow of thinking.

For the final, make sure you do past papers, review your past Labs and PAs, and remember how each algorithm in those questions work, because they can be very useful. However, as the exam was done on Canvas, you cannot use tab on the text editor, and for this semester, we were required to leave comments on our code, which could be very time-consuming, and causing many of us cannot finish the exam.

author: MingHin
comment_content: C++ basic contents.
comment_teaching:
good teaching.
speaks explicitly and always encourage students to answer questions. Cindy teaches at a relatively slow speed so even one is a slow thinker, one can still understand the content. But to what degree of understanding depends on your programming sense.
comment_grading:
Bad grade for me.
There are 2 passing requirements: Overall >= some% AND (final + quiz)>= some%. I met the first requirement but due to my poor mental condition during the final exam, I failed to meet the second requirement and I got an F.
comment_workload:
very heavy workload.
3 very long and demanding programming assignments throughout the semester.
a lab for every week (smaller assignments but still time-consuming).

author: 凍檸茶 (未凍)
