author: rind
title: The first course in C++
comment_content:
1. Intro
2. Basics1, 2; Control 1, 2:
Basic syntax of C++, program control.
----Quiz----
3. Function I:
Function definition, modular programming, passing parameter (PBV and PBR)
4. Array:
1d, 2d, higher dimension arrays, array definition, initialisation, C string (special type of char[])
5. Recursion**:
The hardest topic involving logical thinking
6. Scope
7. Struct
8. Function III
9. Separate-Compilation:
Actually you have to know its application, but the code will not be examined.
10. Pointer**:
The hardest topic involving very clear concepts. lvalue and rvalue, pointers, linked list**, dynamic arrays**, memory leaks**
11. Class:
data-member, member functions, constructors and destructor, mutators and accessors
12. Stack and Queue:
Only Stack is examined this year.

I will say not all topics are new to people who can get into this course. Most of them are just different by syntax. Therefore, please do read every slide of your lecture notes. They are pretty short actually. Do recall everything covered in the quiz as they will ask you tricky questions (like those in COMP1022P). The final exam will focus more on writing codes instead of tricky questions, which is what a COMP course suppose to be :)
comment_teaching:
Cecia is very good at explaining concepts and she has good English with intonations :)

Sometimes she will make some silly mistakes, but that doesn't affect too much.

She will provide more details about labs and PAs during lessons, it will be great if you do it after watching her videos. (In this semester, we can assess all the lecture videos of the 4 sessions)

Btw, there is a piazza for everyone to post questions and everyone can reply. Better check out some questions when you got stuck in PA as many generous students are giving tips :)
comment_grading:
Quiz 8%
Lab 10%
PA 36% (10, 12 14%)
Final 46%

The quiz will examine the basic of C++. Therefore if you take 1021 before, please do more revision.

For the final exam, there are 3 parts and each part last for 45 minutes. You have to code in the Canvas textbox which TAB CANNOT BE USED and YOU HAVE TO SCROLL UP AND DOWN to copy the function prototypes :) (They banned copying)

There consists of at least one coding problem in each part and you have to understand the LONG PASSAGE and write codes. I think they do all these INTENTIONALLY to limit our time. Three tips for people taking this kind of exam later:
1. Write your code in recursive ways (usually iterative way cost more time)
2. Be sure that you can write all function taught in class without much thinking...they examine some functions like deleting linked list which already stated in the lecture notes :)
3. Mark down those important things (like prototypes, data structures...), so that you don't have to scroll up and down :D

Grade and Marks are not released...many students get ANNIHILATED by this exam ;)

----Update----
Assignment: Full
Lab: 8/9 (missing 1 lab)
Quiz: Full
Final: 78/100
Overall marks ~ 88/100
Final grade: A+

Actually I do surprised by the final grade. Since the highest of the final exam is around 92 and I am quite far from it. Better than what I expected :D
comment_workload:
No attendance for class.
1 lab every week, 3 Programming assignments, 1 quiz, 1 final

We have to finish the lab before and demo during lab session. Therefore, you have to finish it quickly and no TA can help you :( (at least for my session)

The difficulty of PA2 will be the highest since it examines the implementation of the hardest logical-demanding topic --> recursion. I know some students takes weeks to finish it. For me it takes around 20-25 hours in total.
The other 2 PA are easier, finish within 1 day.

Workload really depends on your own experience and expectations.

If you take 1021/1022q as the prerequisite, then please pay more attention because C++ is completely different from python and VBA. Especially 1021 teaches turtle??? which is useless in this course...
I think the most difficult things for 1021 students will be the syntax and concepts of OOP. (As python is too easy to use!)

If you take 1022p as the prerequisite, congrats! Those content taught in 1022p are useful in 2011(because it is also OOP). Some topics like "Class" will be easy for you :)

author: Andy Leung
title: Easy to study, hard to get high grade
comment_content: This course teaches basic concepts of C++, such as pointers, simple data types, and classes. The materials taught are very useful in comp2012, which I considered as a sequel of 2011. If you are very interested in c++, I recommend you to reg COMP2012H instead of (COMP2011 + COMP2012)

comment_grading:
Due to online lecturing, the grading system is different from other semesters.
10% - Lab Exercises + Attandance

(8% - pa1, 10% - pa2, 14% - pa3, 12% - pa4)

70/100 - pa1
84/100 - pa2
72/100 - pa3
100/100 - pa4
1/1 - Final exam dry-run
85.5/100 - final

For me, the assignments are somewhat tricky, and easy to lose points due to unexpected reasons. If you want to get 100% for your assignments, the workload is very heavy. Other than that, the workload overall is similar to other COMP courses.

author: NaturalEthan
title: The syllabus can be improved!
comment_content:
Very Basic knowledge about C ++
Course webpage:
https://course.cse.ust.hk/comp2011/web/lectureNotes.html

Syllabus
C++ Basics
Control Flow
Basics II
Control II
Problems for Basics and Control
Function I
Array
Function II: Recursion
Function III: Miscellaneous
Scope
Struct
Separate Compilation
File I/O
Pointer
C++ Class
Stack & Queue ( self study in this semester)
New C++ Features( self study in this semester)
C++ String( self study in this semester)


The content is very basic for beginners. However, the time arrangement is very inappropriate. Some basic features like control flow, array, recursion, etc. have been covered in COMP1022P, COMP1022Q, but they occupied half of the semester's time. However, very important and difficult parts like Pointers and Classes were taught in a short time even if 2 PAs need to applicate them and more than 50% percent credits of the final exam are related to them.
  So what I want to remind you is that if you have time, try to self study this course as fast as you can so then you have more time to deal with Class and Pointer.
comment_teaching: Cecia teaches in a very friendly and interactively way. Her teaching is very clear and vivid. However, if you are confident enough, you can just study by yourself because the slides are elaborated very well.
comment_grading:

author: Yash
comment_content: The content of the course was really interesting. It will teach you the basics of c++ really well. From variables to classes and objects, the content gets interesting as the course proceeds.
comment_teaching: The lecture slides are detailed and very self-explanatory. They have examples for each and every concept. The code files for each example are also uploaded on the course website for assistance.
comment_grading: The grading was based on assignments, labs, and the final exam. There was no midterm exam. It was replaced by an assignment. The grading is very reasonable. All appeals are catered to and inspected carefully. If marks are reduced, the reasons are given specifically. However, the course was really competitive. The online version of the final exam was graded appropriately and marks were given for steps and not just the final code.
comment_workload:
The workload is more than the other courses. Every week, there is a lob where you have to demonstrate the code for the task for that given lab. If you fail to do so properly, marks are deducted. So you have to be prepared before the lab each week.

The assignments consume a lot of time as well. A couple assignments took weeks to finish. There were four assignments. The third one was the hardest.

Additionally, the course content has a lot to learn. Therefore. keeping up with the lectures is important.

author: Justin Li
title: Work-Intensive Programming Course
comment_content:
This courses teaches all the basics about C++ programming and gives brief introduction to OOP (Object-oriented programming). Topics include Basic if-else logics, loops, functions, arrays, recursion, struct, pointers and class. Compared to CSE introductory 1000 courses, this course is much more advanced and demanding towards logical thinking and computation. The topics before mid-term exam are considerably basic while those afterwards are much more vague and complicated, especially with recursion and  pointers which are very logical.
Lab assignments are compulsory and follows closely the contents taught in lecture. Some lab assignments, especially with recursion and pointers quite difficult.
comment_teaching:
Professor Chung was very familiar with the content yet many will find his lectures rather tedious and boring. Those who want interesting and interactive lectures may want to choose one taught by other professors.
All labs and lab assignments are personal work and not are taught by TAs, but they were very welcoming in solving questions and clarifying concepts.
comment_grading: The expectations of this course is high. The mid-term exam was relatively easy with mean around 65%. Meanwhile, only a few students were able to receive over 75% in the final exam, mean of around 45%, and by scoring 50% will get you B range because the final exam was simply very difficult.
comment_workload: Because the way of examining in this course is very different from CSE introductory courses (from filling questions and blanks to writing whole length codes), it took quite a lot of time and work to get used to the style. Revising for vague and complicated topics like recursion and pointers required completing many past exam questions. This is not an easy course, and there is no work around it, so put more time and effort to get good results.

'A' in the end.

author: Raymond Cheng
title: Very good content and teaching
comment_content:
The course teaches about the fundamentals of C++. Here is a snapshot of the syllabus:

Basics (main, namespace, data types, constants)
- Control (For loops, switch statements, enum)
- Functions (declare and call functions, pass by value & by reference, return by value & return by reference)
Struct
- Cpp11 new features (general initialization using {} list, range for statement, lambda expressions)
- Scope (File, function, block scope)
- Pointers
- Arrays
- Recursion
- Class

All of the content is very practical and up-to-date to modern C++. It helped me understand the entire syntax and how C++ works in general.

Everything starting from the basics until functions should be pretty fundamental and easy to understand if you have taken a previous CS introductory course.

Topics from Struct to Scope should be straightforward as well, just pay attention to the specific syntax and how the features are used and you should be fine.

Starting from pointers, things are getting slightly more difficult as you are expected to know how they relate to normal variables and arrays and memory manipulation. For the last few lectures, you need to know how all the concepts come together as you will be expected to implement them in the exams.

Overall the content is great and very appropriate.
comment_teaching: The professor is pretty articulate in explaining concepts that might be pretty difficult to understand at first. He makes things easier to comprehend and demonstrates a lot of code examples in class which will clarify a lot of things. He is also very open to any kind of questions in class, so definitely take the chance in class to ask questions you are not sure of.
comment_grading: Grading is a bit harsh I would say. To my knowledge (not sure whether it is 100% accurate though), midterm mean is around 55 and final mean around 63, and you need at least 25 points above the mean to get an A. Getting the mean should be a B- or C+.

comment_teaching:
The professor speaks in a fast-pace but understandable, which is good. However, he focused on his programming console throughout the lectures which made the class atmosphere uncomfortable for non-CS major students. I skipped most of the lectures except those having in-class quizzes because of that.

The course adopted a new syllabus which focused on students' creativity on programming, making it unideal for students who want to learn more about computer science but not programming.
comment_grading:
Grading Scheme:
Lab exercises 40% (10 total, 4% each)
Mean almost 100%
Quizzes 15% (In-class, open-book, 3 total, 5% each)
Mean: 84.1/100 (SD 19.3), 87.5/100 (SD 11.8), 74.1/100 (SD 20.0) (exclude zeros)
Final Exam 45% (Open-book)
Mean 56.2/100, SD 18.4 (exclude zeros)

The course grade was curved in a ridiculous way, I got almost full marks on labs, below mean on quizzes and below mean on final (~50%), total score was about 68-69%, final grade was a C.
A friend of mine literally scored more than half of my final exam scores (~25-30%), and he got the same grade as me (C). This is totally unfair as the final exam was the biggest deciding factor, the curve was not correlated to the final exam at all, which does not make any sense. I am actually rather disappointed and frustrated when receiving the grades.
comment_workload:
There are lab exercises weekly, which takes on average 4-5 hours to complete, however some labs involving pointers, linked lists are quite challenging, these would take several days to complete. So most of us have to finish the lab exercises before the lab as lab problems are posted online a week before. I personally skipped most of the lab sections and submitted the exercises on Canvas.
Quizzes are not heavy loads for me as I gave up all of them and all in final instead.
The final exam is quite challenging so it takes much time to prepare for. As I had the MATH 2033 exam one day before, there was not too much time to prepare the exam for the COMP 2011 and I suffered from it.
In all, the workload of the course is quite heavy, especially for labs involving higher-level knowledge such as pointers and linked lists.

author: Sunny0516
title: Fundamental CS course
comment_content:
This course will teach fundamental CS and OOP concepts by using C++, including functions / array / scope / structures / pointer / class / stack and queue / new C++11 features (added this semester).

For pointers, we have to learn about linked lists too, and of course these will come along with recursion (functions), dynamic array, memory allocation and leakage.

The regular 11 labs were about:
1 - Basic C++
2 - Basic I/O
3 - Nested Loops
4 - 1D array
5 - 2D array
6 - Recursion
7 - File I/O and string (not accessed in final exam)
8 - Structures
9 - Pointer
10 - Dynamic array and linked list
11 - Classes

Midterms accessed you from basic C++ to functions (recursion, overload, pass by reference, declaration and definition)

Final accessed everything except file I/O, C++ strings and queue structure type.
comment_teaching:
Cecia is one of the best tutors I have ever met. She can teach many things not mentioned on lecture notes, which is helpful for you to clear concepts. She will also demonstrate and explain about the examples given on lecture notes (which unfortunately lacked explanation)

After lessons, students could still approach her easily. You can even find her after lesson times to ask about this course's materials.
comment_grading:
Grading scheme is as follows:
Quiz at beginning of semester: 5%
Regular lab practice every week: 10%
Programming assignments * 3: 24% (6+8+10)
Midterm: 25%
Final Exam: 36%


Quiz: Full Mark 25, Mean 20.00, SD 2.91.

Lab: Basically everyone got full marks.

PA: Most people got 80 or above for the 3 assignments. The guidelines were not given explicitly, but as long as you follow the requirements strictly, you should be able to get fair marks for the 3 assignments.

Midterm: Mean 60.1, SD 18.5. 4 past papers from recent years will be given on the course site, which can allow you to preview what to expect in the exam. The exam was not too difficult, and people could get up to 99/100 marks in this semester.

Final: Mean 55.4, SD 22.0. Similar to midterms, you can get 4 past papers from recent years too. You were given 3 hours to complete 11 questions (first question consist of 10 T/F parts), and questions related to pointers / linked lists were fairly difficult.



The grading was quite fair I think. I got 92.5% (above 1.8 SD in midterm, 1.7 SD in final) in total and resulted in A+; people claimed that getting above mean can already give you a B+.
comment_workload:
The workload for this course is quite large, even for a 4 credit course. There are 3 PAs, 11 labs, midterm and final exam for this course.

For the first 3-4 labs, you only have to spend around 30 minutes each, but for the later labs, you may end up spending above 1 day to complete the lab, which was a waste of time. However, as the coding for the labs would not be checked, many people ended up copying others' code for the regular labs.

For the 3 PAs, the difficulty grew exponentially - the first PA was fairly easy and could be finished in 2 hours, while the 3rd PA took me more than 3 days to finish.

You had to go through all lecture notes thoroughly for the midterm and final exam.

comment_workload: The pre-requisite for this course is a basic 1000-level COMP course. There is a lot of effort, and avoid taking this course if you have a lot of credits for the semester. The syllabus till before the midterm is basically just recursion and loops. However, after midterm, we are introduced to arrays, pointers, classes and objects, stacks and queues, linked lists etc.

author: hhh_caroline
comment_content: The tutorial sessions are enjoyable and provide a welcoming introduction to programming in C++. The instructor creates a lively and interactive learning environment, which makes the material more approachable. However, the curriculum is somewhat limited in scope, focusing primarily on fundamental concepts such as basic C++ syntax, pointers, classes, and stack operations. While these topics are essential for beginners, the tutorial could benefit from covering more advanced or practical applications to better prepare students for real-world programming challenges. Expanding the content to include topics like dynamic memory management, inheritance, or common data structures would greatly enhance its educational value. Despite this, the tutorial remains a useful starting point for those new to C++
comment_teaching: This course introduces core C++ programming concepts, including pointers, classes, and stack data structures. While lecture notes provide key explanations, students are encouraged to engage in additional self-directed learning to fully master the topics.
comment_grading: As is widely recognized, achieving a high grade requires considerable effort and dedication
comment_workload: The assignments before the midterm are fairly easy, but after the midterm, they become more challenging and require extra time and effort. However, they’re a great way to deepen your understanding and apply what you’ve learned. You’ll actually enjoy working on them! Plus, the difficulty is manageable since you have unlimited attempts to practice and improve.

author: LeeGyumin
comment_content: The content of the C++ programming course was generally quite good. It covered all the important concepts and topics related to C++ programming, including variables, data types, control structures, functions, classes, and object-oriented programming. The course materials, including textbooks and lecture notes, were comprehensive and provided a solid foundation for understanding the language. However, there were a few areas where the content could have been improved, such as providing more real-world examples and practical applications of C++ programming.
comment_teaching: The teaching in the C++ programming course was satisfactory. The instructor had a good understanding of the subject matter and was able to explain the concepts clearly in class. They were knowledgeable and approachable, and encouraged students to ask questions and seek clarification. The instructor also provided helpful resources, such as coding exercises and programming challenges, to reinforce the learning. However, there were times when the pace of the lectures was a bit fast, making it challenging to fully grasp some of the complex topics.
comment_grading: The grading in the C++ programming course was average. The criteria for grading were communicated at the beginning of the course, and the instructor provided timely feedback on assignments and tests. However, there were instances where the grading seemed inconsistent, with some assignments receiving higher grades than expected based on the feedback provided. Additionally, the grading rubrics could have been more detailed and transparent to ensure a fair assessment of students' work.
comment_workload: The workload in the C++ programming course was quite heavy and overwhelming. There were numerous assignments, projects, and coding exercises to complete within tight deadlines. The workload often required long hours of coding and debugging, leaving little time for other coursework or personal activities. It would have been beneficial if the workload had been more balanced and manageable, allowing students to delve deeper into the concepts without feeling overly burdened.

comment_workload:
The heaviness of the workload for a course varies from person to person, it might not apply to you. From my point of view, the workload is not heavy for a four-credit course.

First of all, the 2 hours online labs are one-by-one questioning sessions about your weekly lab code, which a TA will ask about how your code works. It doesn't last that long; you can leave after answering the questions or leave immediately if you're not picked for answering questions.

For programming assignments, a skeleton code will be provided, and you are only required to implement certain functions, which is only around 10 to 30 lines long for each function.

comment_teaching:

comment_grading:
Not yet release.

Marks gain from:
1.Exam
2.Assignment
3.Tutorial
4.Quiz

For 2 and 3, it is not difficult to get all the marks.
For 4, it's a normal quiz.

For 1, it's a disaster to us.
In this semester, we take the exam on Canvas. The profs says that it should be more easier as we can type using keyboard. BUT, it just a joke.

For the short question, we just need to read the code and write the output. It's ok if it's not some long code.

For the long question, it's insane. The question is like a long eassy pasted on the Canvas. The question is listed at the top and the answer box is listed at the bottom. You will need to scroll downward  at least 2 - 3 times (using your mouse wheel) to reach the answer box. The question ask you to fill your answer in the blank. But, actually you need to copy the whole things from the question to your answer box. However, the question is not a text that you can copy, but an image. So, you need to scroll up 3 times, remember it, scroll down 3 times, write it, fill in your answer, scroll up 3 times, check it again, scroll down 3 times, check it again. Then you finish the part a) (i). That mean you need to repeat it for many many many time to answer a single long question.

It seems that it is not my personal problem as the mean is really low even though the question is not that difficult.

For the Exam, the mean is 39.8.
For the Assignments, the mean is always around 8x% while there are always >200 students in my lecture group get full marks.

So, I truely hope that you don't need to take the exam on Canvas.

Unlimited HomeWork.

author: JinWan
title: Its a gud course
comment_content:
Took the course during the first semester through c-placement test. If you have any prior experience coding, even if not in c, I recommend you take this course rather than 1021 which is quite boring - unless you want a strong grade boost. Comp 2011 is an actual programming course. It goes over more complex computational thinking concepts such as recursion, pointers, and object-oriented programming (although the last doesn't go too in-depth). In my personal opinion, the course is pretty well thought out as well. You will have to do multiple lab assignments (most of which can be finished in an hour, maximum like 4 hours for people who've coded before). The longer extended assignments (the PAs) are a lot more complex, and also a lot more interesting to work on as you are actually coding something relevant (war simulator, minesweeper).

Besides the labs, there are the usual midterm and final, with finals taking 50% of the entire course grade.
comment_teaching: Its alright, all the bases covered. Prof CHUNG, Albert tries his best to keep the class engaged by breaking down concepts more simply. His teaching style is a lot more on-the-white board, but his English is plenty decent and you can understand everything as well.
comment_grading: The grading is pretty typical UST, nothing exceptional. I got 2stdv above the median for midterms, and 1.5 stdv above the median for finals, with a final grade of A.
comment_workload: If you have prior coding experience the workload is extremely low compared to other courses. I was able to cruise through most of the course without much problems. Attendance isn't mandatory, though the weekly lab assignments are. However, those are really not hard, and actually quite enjoyable to work on anyways. No complaints. If you haven't coded that much besides the pre-requisite courses, I don't have much of a reference point but I don't think it will be THAT much homework.

author: desmond fanboy
title: EZ for CS students
comment_content:
Starts off with basic C++ syntax: if statements, loops, functions, etc.

Pointers and references might be slightly tricky, but with some practice they should be fine.

Recursion is another topic where many people struggle. Try googling "recursive leap of faith," and follow that technique. Also useful for COMP 2711 or 2711H when you need to use recursion.

Stacks and queues are also relatively straight forward, try to solve some coding questions online to get a better sense of them.

If you have learned Java before, the classes section covered in COMP 2011 should be a piece of cake.
comment_teaching: I attended only a few of his lectures because I had already known much of the content, but I can say that Gary is a very passionate instructor. Occasionally, Gary would delve deep into the memory aspect of computing and show us what's actually happening under the hood in the memory level. Quite interesting and useful if you are a CS major.
comment_grading:
Grading is mainly determined by your midterm and final grades as most people earn 100s for their labs and PAs. Personally, I kinda screwed my midterm because I had spent too much time double checking the first half of the questions, and barely had enough time to complete the harder questions towards the end. The final was harder than the midterm in terms of difficulty, but as long as you can think logically and write your algorithms by hand, you should be able to get a high score.

Midterm: 0.8 sd above mean

author: chronoaero
title: If you are interested in COMP, please give this course a try... good grade course (Don't go to his section if possible tho)
comment_content:
I am writing this as a first year student (and someone who needs access to reviews with course code >2000 badly). I am previously assigned to be enrolled in COMP1021. I have learnt some programming before, so I am a bit unsure whether relearning if else and loops from scratch again is an enjoyable experience...

Oh wait - out of nowhere CSE is sending an email at the start of the semester offering a C placement test to get a requisite waiver for COMP 2011. I'm interested in exploring what's in COMP anyway... so.. why not? I thought.

Anyway, this course does cover more than just learning a new programming language. C++ is called a low-level (?) programming language for a reason. Please expect that you are not only learning new syntaxes in this course.

[Contents before midterm]
- C++ Basic and Control Flow (Might be a repetition of COMP1021 (?), but now you have a slightly 'strange' syntax. They also have something called reference variable (they will usually put this quite annoying thing in the exam))
- Functions (Note that they are introducing passing and RETURN (some people might miss this) by reference here)
- Arrays (More or less like python lists)
- Recursion
- Scope (Basically which variable do you use if you have more than one definition of variable (yes, they might also test this in a very annoying way))
- Struct

(Note that function overloading may be included in the first half (as seen from the given past papers, but they did not include it this year))

[Contents after midterm]
- Function Overloading and Declaration (Yes, definition and declaration is different)
# Pointers (Basically the scariest and the main part of this course; they will also discuss how to manage memory and introduce memory leak here. You will start to understand how memory is allocated in programs after learning this part)
- Class (Note that they are also introducing something called constructor and deconstructor here)
- Stack and Queue

- New C++ features (not included in the exam this year, includes stuff like lambda expressions and stuff that might not really be used in actual programming. But you can still read them if you got the time)
comment_teaching:
Personally, If possible, I am not sure how to rate my instructor... I can see that he is trying to explain the topics and is really open for questions, but I find the explanation is a little bit confusing. However, I find that the slides from the instructors are really helpful, and does the job to help you understand the topic. I still come to the lecture to make sure what I might have missed.

==========================================
Edit 1 semester after the review is posted and I finished COMP2012 >:) : ok... let's update on who's my instructor haha..

Sorry Prof I need to give you bad rating although the grade is good ... don't want to mislead people who are not familiar with C++ :(

Umm.. you may want to read the review of COMP3021 to know what's the problem... but honestly since other instructors are the one who are mainly in charge of the class.. there aren't that many "accidents" in his COMP2011.

I admit that the class is slightly disorganized... and what I did in class as I remember is try to implement what he is talking about in lecture at my own pace. He did not really give that much examples that he coded himself which may make some topics confusing. He just writes annotations on the lecture slide which I think is a bad practice. However, I think he actually pretty knowledgeable in C++, he explained things like reference to arrays (2012 stuff) and struct can have member function. It's probably just his teaching... let's give him some justice and hope it'll improve. If you get his class, though.. just make examples at your own pace like I did and maybe ask questions directly to him. But if you can attend other sessions... maybe do.

I recommend someone like Prof Cindy or Prof Cecia (my 2012 lecturer, her class is SO organized UwU)

due to the quite confusing state of the lecture, let's give that a C (considering there is E, this is mean grade), but because the TA is nice I'll give the TA team an A.. so on average: B

I do hope the lecture can improve considering that he seems to know a lot more about C++ than it seems on the surface...
=======================================

I might suggest going to L(smaller numbers), e.g. L1, L2, L3 if possible, though. If you can't, you may try reading the slides more before class, and probably ask the instructors or post a question on piazza.

I find that the TAs for this course are really active and ready to answer your questions. There was one time when I sent a question via email to the TA about the PA, and I received a response in less than 10 minutes. The discussion on Piazza is also really active, you will get response really fast (except for the few days before the finals, which is understandable)
comment_grading:
I think the grading has a fair combination of coding with compiler and actually testing you on whether or not you understand C++ well i.e., code without compiler

Labs (12%)
Works this way:
Each lab allows you to get 3 points at maximum

You need to do the task given and send your code to ZINC before the deadline. Then, you need to attend the zoom session with the TA:

- If you are chosen to answer questions from the TA:
Your attendance in the zoom is worth 1 point, answering the TA's question is worth 1 point, and your ZINC grading is worth 1 point

- Otherwise:
Your attendance in the zoom is worth 1 point, and your ZINC grading is worth 2 points. (You might not want this when the lab is quite hard)

The lab usually takes about an hour or so to do. If it is a bit harder, maybe about two hours.

3 PAs (24%)
Basically lab but harder. Do note that for PA3, it is usually really challenging as they have pointers already taught in class.

Each PA might take about 3-5 hours to do, start early.

Midterm (24%)
Finals (40%)

My Grades & Statistics:
Labs: Mostly 3/3 except for one time I missed a testcase and got 2.8/3

PAs:
PA1: 100/100 [Mean: 90.67 Median: 98.55 Standard deviation: 16.84]
PA2: 100/100 [Mean: 90.31 Median: 100.00 Standard deviation: 22.88] (Yes, the median is full mark)
PA3: 100/100 [Mean: 71.31 Median: 80.00 Standard deviation: 29.60]

Midterm: 96.75/100 [Mean: 61.18 Median: 61.75 Stdev: 20.31] (Code on paper)

Finals: 87.25/100 [Mean: 54.51 Median: 54.63 Standard deviation: 22.32] (Also, code on paper)

Grade: A+

I suggest that you should come to the paper checking session, you may be able to squeeze out some points.
comment_workload:
Generally, I think the workload is ok. But saying that it is very light is deceiving.

Please remember to take your time to 'stress test' your labs and PAs. Passing the given testcases might not be enough, try to think about some crazy testcase that they might throw on your code and break it. Also, watch out that ZINC is running on linux and sometimes unitialized variables contains garbage value unlike and might produce behaviour that windows does not produce. Run your code on some linux-based compiler online and it will do the work. You might want to try github codespaces (my instructor suggested us to use this around the start of the term as some people may not have g++ installed). Try to squeeze out marks from the PA as this is a chance for you to program with a compiler.

For the exams, practice coding on paper once in a while. You may try something like LeetCode and try their easy problems on paper. If you can do it, generally, you should be fine for the test.

author: SVM

author: ycohui
title: Learn C again with C++ syntax
comment_content:
The content is really boring, from a student who learnt C language already for years before. (and this semester we got so bipolar level of students. Fun Note: This semester COMP2011 students from MATH department had the fail rate up to 30%)

Which those students also slowed down the pace so much, we don't even have time to cover Queue, Stack and Lambda (not even in COMP2012 Fall23).

However, before rushing to enrolling to COMP2011 at Spring semester. I must told you that the grading in A range is really hash.
See grading for details.
comment_teaching:
Cecia is a such good lecturer. She had adjust the pace of teaching in order to cater different students paces. Although she never use hotkey and sometimes messed up the code in demos, the overall lessons is great and not boring.

One extra note, she would happily to answer the questions from everyone, please feel free to ask her after the lesson, As the content is "rich" in terms of the syllabus, and the questions in exams is CLS (crazy).
comment_grading:
As above, although the grade distribution seems more lenient in Spring Semester than Fall Semester, I must warn you that the A-range is really limited and competitive. As there are many future CS/CPEG students with much prior knowledge taking this course in spring semester (aka me), there are limited A+ to give out.

For DSE ICT students references:
I got friends who studied Lang C in DSE with decent grade, still find a bit harsh for the mid-terms and finals. The final grade was B-range only.
Please consider before enrolling into the course.

Grades:
My grade are useless for references, you probably gets A+ in a 300 ppl course if you were several marks from the highest.
comment_workload:
The workload is really depends on your level of knowledge.

8 Online Labs & 3 Programming Assignments & 2 Exams

Labs are doable within an hour, generally. (Maybe not in the topic of recursion / pointer). Please expect 100% in all these Labs.

3 PAs, where the workload of the course came from.
Most of the time goes to debugging, expect two days or so to finish it for the PA2/3 for most of the students.
PA1: C++ Usage
PA2: Recursion
PA3: Pointers (Probably Linked List)

Exams, not workload(?)
The exam are "hard", you really need to know the details fully to able get the marks in the exam. This would drive the students who first studied in C++ crazy, as they need these to separate the tops students. Also, Import note: The exam is Pen-and-Paper, no computer allowed, please debug with your brain.

That's it.

author: Ahhim
title: Need to work hard on self study
comment_content: Include basic of C++. From simple things like For-loop, while-loop to the later harder topics like recursion and pointers. Pointers are really complicated and confusing in my opinion so better work harder on that before it is too late.
comment_teaching: The instructors and TAs are quite nice. The lectures are not boring and the professor always explains with examples, which is easier to understand imo.
comment_grading:
reasonable grading. 8 Labs and 3 assignments all came with which specific tasks you failed so you can appeal if you think there is any mistakes

The Labs and homework are not that hard to do and you can always find TA for help if you do not know how you do. Just remember they are the easy source for scores.

Midterms and Finals are rather difficult compare to them. Since it requires you to write code by hand, I think time limit is quite a concern.
comment_workload:
As I mentioned in the previous part, there are 8 labs and 3 homework. The labs are all quite simple and will not take too much of  your time to finish them, even if you are not familiar with the topics. The assignments on the other hand are quite time-consuming as it consists of numerous of complex tasks.

You usually have either a homework or lab per week.

author: PakNin
title: Not a very difficult course
comment_content:
This course covers C++ from basics to detailed OOP. The first half of the course is easy, just simple indentation and logical thinking(if else, while, switch), which are similar to COMP1021. The second half of the course started to be a bit challenging, which covers pointers, class and stacks. They would be the boss in final exam.
Here are the content list.
1 Introduction
1-2 C++ Basics
1-2 Control Flow
1-2 Problems for Basics and Control
3-4 Function I
5 Array
6 Function II: Recursion  
6 Scope
7 Struct
7-8 Function III: Miscellaneous  
9-11 Pointer
11-12 C++ Class
13 Stack & Queue
and some self-study topics.
comment_teaching:
Cecia is good at teaching, her English is easy to understand, and various detailed example programs are provided.
The arrangement of lab sessions was not ideal. Some students have to sit in the Zoom lesson and wait for about an hour so as to answer Q&A from TAs
For me, I am quite familiar with C++ so I skipped most of the lessons after the first few weeks.
comment_grading:
Grading scheme is a bit different from 1021, which not only require your marks to be greater than a constant, but with this formula,
(total score >= X) AND ((weighted midterm and final exam scores) >= Y), where X and Y are constants.

Personally I think grading is fair.
Lab:         10/10 (10 Labs, only count highest 8 labs)
PA:           23/24 (3 PA, each 8%)
MidTerm:  24/26
Exam:       32.12/40

Overall:     89.12/100
Grade:      A
comment_workload:
4 cred course so workload is a bit high. Each week one lab starting from week 1, but students only need to attend every other Zoom lab sessions to answer Q&A(still have to submit lab assignment). The labs were easy, guidelines were provided tho not as detailed as 1021. Mostly can finish in 30-45 mins.

PA is difficult and time-consuming. They were released after midterm exam. They mostly test your understanding of specific topics(e.g. recursion, pointers, class...) but not copying sample programs. Mostly need days to solve it. (Goop is your friend, some nice guys may post hints there)

author: ctliaj
comment_content:
In general
Basics: if statement/ for loop/ while loop, scope/ datatypes/ struct/ OOP
Concept: Recursion, Pointer, implementation of data structures (linked list, stack, queue, and Binary tree)

The harder topic in the course is Pointer, which took a big part in my final exam. For those who want to prepare in advance, I recommend starting with Pointer.
comment_teaching:
It was surprising that she was able to go through all the lecture materials so much in-depth and demonstrate all the programs given on the course website multiple times only in a period of three months. Some say she'd explain the fundamental even the trivial concepts too much in detail thus making the course boring.

Other than that, there is nothing wrong with the way she teaches. It was quite helpful and concise actually, even for students with no prior programming experience.
comment_grading:
Lab exercises (10%) 10/10, once every week

3 programming assignments (10%, 12%, 15%)
pa1 74/100 (Manipulating elements in an array),
mean 88/100
Comment: Not hard at all. The -36 marks was me forgetting to delete a line of printout.

pa2 100/100 (Finding the longest path with recursion)
mean 71
Comment: Hardest of all. Regardless, the majority got 100/100.

pa3 100/100 (Doubly linked list represented by a train)
mean 84
Comment: Easier than pa2. Very straightforward.

Quiz (10%) 31/35. Include only the basics written above up to the topic of Datatypes. The hardest part for me was to trace the program.

Final exam (43%) 56.5/100. Most questions were about OOP with pointers. It was mostly about solving problems rather than just coding. One of mine was a question from Leetcode. Nevertheless, doing every past paper on the course website and reading the exam questions carefully should be sufficient for getting >70%.

Overall ~77/100 B+

People with overall ~mean get B. The cut-off is probably at 80% like most of the other comp courses.
comment_workload:
One assignment per month. Although each can be done in probably less than a week.

Ten labs in total, a couple of hours should be enough.

I think the workload is pretty light!

author: yeahmoee
title: Pretty well designed course
comment_content: The content is very well-rounded.  At the start of the course, you will be taught the very basics of C++, then they will move onto more advanced topics such as Pointers and Class. In general, this course is a very good start for CS students.
comment_teaching: Cindy is an awesome lecturer. Her English is good. She gives interesting analogies to difficult ideas. She is also willing to explain to students after the class or in-office hours.
comment_grading:
Grading weight: 10 Labs (only 9 for me due to holiday policy), 3 PAs, 1 Quiz, 1 Final

10 Labs (10 %)
1 Quiz (10 %)
3 PAs (~40 %)
Final (~40 %)

In general, this course's grading is pretty average. Getting an A-range grade is difficult, though it is not impossible. My suggestion is to start doing your PAs as early as possible, they are all very difficult, especially PA2 (recursion) and PA3 (pointers), and when it is around the due date, the submission platform Zinc takes very long to grade. The labs are fairly easy. The Quiz mainly focused on C++ syntax and control flows, and it is easy as well. The final is really, really hard though, it is an overview of what you learned throughout the semester.
comment_workload: You should expect to spend 1-2 hours for labs, and 8-10 hours for PAs (normally finished in 3-5 days). You should expect to spend at least 5-10 hours a week on the course materials. It is not that bad.

author: JustinIsVerySex
title: Apparently this is a "hard" course.
comment_content: If you barely had to study for COMP 1021 you would have little to no issue with the content. It is really intuitive and easy to follow for the first half of the semester. It takes a tad bit more effort in the later chapters but it is nothing to sweat about. Most of the things you can just understand from a glance and only requires a bit of thinking to implement. Among all the courses I studied this semester this one requires the least effort.
comment_teaching: Cecia Chan is energetic when she teaches and gives clear examples when explaining new concepts. At the start of each lesson, quizzes the class on what they learnt last time to make sure they remember. Nothing really much to criticize.
comment_grading:
Consistency is key with this course with roughly 30% of the grade being assignments and weekly labs. The exam questions are only a little tough but your real enemy is time. You have to balance writing your code flawlessly vs writing your code quickly.


The only truly annoying thing is you have to write your code on paper, which is really dumb, because it is easy to make syntax mistake and hard to rewrite code once you found a problem. You have to really, really think clearly about your code before you write it down, or otherwise there is not enough space.
comment_workload:
The weekly labs are not difficult, only requiring you to have fundamental knowledge to understand how to do. For earlier labs you can finished them in >= 30 minutes, and at most, when lab content is about pointers, they can take 3 hours at most, 1 if you are smart about it.

The assignments on the other hand have many little tasks that require more thinking. There are only 3 in the whole semester, but you put in ~ 3 days for the last one. If you want to get full assignment points, make sure to allocate time because the final assignment is close to finals, where you might have a ton of other stuff to do.

author: 無愁子
title: The first useful coding course in UST
comment_content:
This course focuses on a systematic view of C++. Unlike the useless COMP 1021, this course provides you with detailed step by step about the operation of C++ as a programming language. While the lecture notes are very detailed, and if u focus on every single slide you can handle >90% of the question, you will get a much better understanding if you go take the lecture. In the lecture, on top of what the lecture notes cover (mostly only describing the behaviour), Brian will explain and provide some alternative solutions if u want to attain another behaviour, and more importantly, the logic behind the decision of this behaviour.
Therefore, I would highly suggest you go and take the lecture or watch the video afterwards.
comment_teaching: As mentioned in the content part, I think Brian teaches well, he explains the logic behind it in detail and is willing to leave it behind after every lecture and answer all questions. Moreover, his office hours have quite less people in fact (as he is only responsible for one lecture only), so he can have more time to solve your issues. Anyway, he is a good instructor.
comment_grading:
The grading scheme is as below:

Lab exercises 10%
Programming assignments 24% (8%, 8%, 8%)
Quiz 5%
Midterm exam   25%
Final exam 36%
Total      100%

Please note that the grading scheme only takes 66% of the exams, your weighted exam scores (quiz + MT + Final) need to be >~ 30% on top of having a total of >~ 50% to get a pass.

My Scores:
Labs (8 labs, each of them score 3): 24/24
PA: 100% + 98% + 93.29%
Quiz: 22/24
Mid Term: 95.5/100
Final: 98.2/100
Grade: A+

While I got 97.2% to get an A+, it is not necessary to score that high to get an A+, as far as I know, should be around 93% would be fine.
comment_workload:
I do agree the workload of the PA would be a bit high for people who have less experience in coding, but basically, each PA will have 2 weeks to do, so I think it is somehow fair. A suggestion is to handle the PA2 as long as it is released and go find a TA or friend if you have an issue during development, as the lab of the second half is difficult.

Suggestions for ppl who want to score high in PA:
In PA2, they do not explicitly say they will do `sanitiser` and `valgrind` so I got a score deduction due to missing that part (I accessed the uninitialised value of an array while it will not lead to undefined behaviour but still got score deduction -> follow the rule for uninitialised value)
In PA3, I made a wrong assumption about the behaviour and so I got several tests wrong directly, if they don't provide a simulator, ask in the piazza for all unclear scenarios and never make assumptions.

author: Vegetable
title: Decent Programming Course as Intro to COMP
comment_content: Please refer to other posts for details. Remember to pay close attention to recursion as it is very useful on handling certain kinds of questions and occurs frequently in both pastpapers and the final of 2021_Spring. The curriculum setting is very fundamental and easy compared to similar courses in other school, so for the students interested in more advanced and challenging topics, you need to spend some time searching for exercises and projects yourselves.
comment_teaching: Special Credit To Cindy! The pace of her lectures is pretty slow honestly, but she can explain every single concept clearly and also weclomes any question at any time. If she does not remember the exact answer for some very detailed questions about C++ standard, she will reply you the answer after checking the relative documents herself. I am also glad that I get a responsible and patient TA on my lab session. The overall experience as a student of COMP2011 is perfect.
comment_grading: Please refer to other posts for details. I think the grading of this semester is pretty nice compared to past ones'. Getting almost fullmark on PA and Lab is a necessary but not sufficient condition for most students aiming for A+, unless you do a really good job on final. For the rest of students, don't get too nervous on final. As long as you do the lab and PA BY YOURSELF, I think at least A range is secured. In this semester's final, about half of students are below 40/100. Comparing it to the statistics of PAs, I have sufficient reasons to believe that a large portion of them did not finish the labs and PAs independently. As the exercises provided by the teaching team are far less than enough, please make use of every chance you get, not just wasting them by "Legal Communication To Other Students". According to my friends' and my scores, the overall cutoff between A+ and A should be around 87.
comment_workload: As I already said in CONTENT part, the workload of this course is pretty light as the lab and PA are really easy, i mean really. The time I spent on checking my PA submissions is twice more than the time spent on writing them (though it is actually not necessary). There is a special point worth being mentioned: write all your codes on txt file and force yourself finish them onethrough because in the exam, you have no opportunity to check your codes by compiling them and no hints about syntax will be given. If you cannot overcome the anxiety during the exam due to the above reasons, you may be crushed and get a suck score. In f2f mode, the coding part is very likely to be hand-written, which also take you some time to get familiar with.

author: skim
comment_content:
Title: Navigating the C++ Challenge: A Major Struggle

Decided to dive into a C++ course as part of my major, thinking it would complement my academic journey. Little did I know, it was like stepping into a coding battlefield.

At first, it seemed innocent enough – for loops, while loops, and the intricacies of C++ syntax. I thought, "Okay, this is manageable." But soon, we were thrust into the deep end, grappling with complex concepts that felt like they belonged in a coding expert's toolkit, not a major student's.

The lack of a gradual learning curve was glaring. Going from fundamental coding to the intricacies of C++ felt like trying to sprint before learning to walk. Even as a major student, it was a challenging leap. C++, with its reputation for complexity, proved to be a formidable opponent.

From tackling turtle graphics from python to wrestling with dynamic double linked structures and delving into object-oriented programming, the course felt like an uphill battle. A major in the subject didn't shield me from the difficulty; if anything, it intensified the struggle.

In essence, the course structure seemed to overlook the principle of learning progression. It's like expecting someone to master a language without teaching them the alphabet first. For those contemplating this course as part of their major, brace yourselves – it's a challenging ride that might leave you questioning your coding prowess.
comment_teaching: I listened lectures from Professor Dimitris, and I must say, his dedication to explanation is commendable. While I find his teaching immensely helpful in grasping the concepts, the real challenge surfaces when attempting problems independently. Despite the thorough understanding I
comment_grading:
Despite diligent preparation, the recent exam posed unexpected challenges, leaving me feeling unprepared despite my efforts. However, working through past papers emerged as a saving grace. These resources not only boosted my confidence but also provided practical insights into the exam format and question types.

In the face of the daunting exam, tackling past papers proved invaluable. It offered clarity on content intricacies and honed my problem-solving skills. Though the journey was tough, the strategic use of past papers provided a sense of progress amid uncertainties. Regardless of the final outcome, the lessons learned and skills developed in this process are undeniably valuable.
comment_workload: Every week, the constant stream of labs and programming assignments is eating up a significant chunk of my time. It's become so overwhelming that I can't even find the space to hang out with friends or focus on other subjects. Struggling to strike a balance, it feels like these tasks are taking over, leaving little room for a healthy work-life equilibrium.
